29 (6) (13) As used in this section:
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
260
1 (a) "Dyslexia" means both of the following:
2 (i) A specific learning disorder that is neurobiological in
3 origin and characterized by difficulties with accurate or fluent
4 word recognition and by poor spelling and decoding abilities that
5 typically result from a deficit in the phonological component of
6 language that is often unexpected in relation to other cognitive
7 abilities and the provision of effective classroom instruction.
8 (ii) A specific learning disorder that may include secondary
9 consequences, such as problems in reading comprehension and a
10 reduced reading experience that can impede the growth of vocabulary
11 and background knowledge and lead to social, emotional, and
12 behavioral difficulties.
13 (b) "Evidence-based" means an activity, program, process,
14 service, strategy, or intervention that demonstrates statistically
15 significant effects on improving pupil outcomes or other relevant
16 outcomes and that meets at least both of the following:
17 (i) At least 1 of the following:
18 (A) Is based on strong evidence from at least 1 well-designed
19 and well-implemented experimental study.
20 (B) Is based on moderate evidence from at least 1 well-
21 designed and well-implemented quasi-experimental study.
22 (C) Is based on promising evidence from at least 1 well-
23 designed and well-implemented correlational study with statistical
24 controls for selection bias.
25 (D) Demonstrates a rationale based on high-quality research
26 findings or positive evaluation that the activity, program,
27 process, service, strategy, or intervention is likely to improve
28 pupil outcomes or other relevant outcomes.
29 (ii) Includes ongoing efforts to examine the effects of the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
261
1 activity, program, process, service, strategy, or intervention.
2 (c) "Explicit" means direct and deliberate instruction through
3 continuous pupil-teacher interaction that includes teacher
4 modeling, guided practice, and independent practice.
5 (d) "Fluency" means the ability to read with speed, accuracy,
6 and proper expression.
7 (e) "Multi-tiered system of supports" means a comprehensive
8 framework that includes 3 distinct tiers of instructional support
9 and is composed of a collection of evidence-based strategies
10 designed to meet the individual needs and assets of a whole pupil
11 at all achievement levels.
12 (f) "Phonemic awareness" means the conscious awareness of all
13 of the following:
14 (i) Individual speech sounds, including, but not limited to,
15 consonants and vowels, in spoken syllables.
16 (ii) The ability to consciously manipulate through, including,
17 but not limited to, matching, blending, segmenting, deleting, or
18 substituting, individual speech sounds described in subparagraph
19 (i).
20 (iii) All levels of the speech sound system, including, but not
21 limited to, word boundaries, rhyme recognition, stress patterns,
22 syllables, onset-rime units, and phonemes.
23 (g) "Phonological" means relating to the system of contrastive
24 relationships among the speech sounds that constitute the
25 fundamental components of a language.
26 (h) "Progress monitoring" means the assessing of students'
27 academic performance, quantifying students' rates of improvement or
28 progress toward goals, and determining how students are responding
29 to instruction.
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
262
1 (i) "Rapid automatized naming (RAN)" means a task that
2 measures how quickly individuals can name objects; pictures;
3 colors; or symbols, including letters and digits, aloud, which can
4 predict later reading abilities for preliterate children.
5 Sec. 35d. (1) From the state school aid fund money
6 appropriated under section 11, there is allocated for 2026-2027
7 only an amount not to exceed $1,000,000.00 for the department to
8 provide grants to districts and intermediate districts for the
9 purchase of 1 or more components or trainings through an eligible
10 teacher training program for children with dyslexia from a provider
11 of an eligible teacher training program for children with dyslexia
12 as described in this section. It is intended that supports funded
13 under this section assist with the implementation of dyslexia
14 requirements under section 1280f of the revised school code, MCL
15 380.1280f.
16 (2) A provider that provides programming that meets all of the
17 following criteria is considered to be a provider of an eligible
18 teacher training program for purposes of this section:
19 (a) The programming allows teachers to incorporate the 5
20 components essential to an effective reading program into their
21 daily lessons. The 5 components described in this subdivision are
22 phonemic awareness, phonics, vocabulary, fluency, and
23 comprehension.
24 (b) The programming trains educators to teach reading using a
25 proven, multisensory approach.
26 (c) The programming educates teachers on how to explicitly and
27 effectively teach reading to beginning readers.
28 (d) The programming breaks reading and spelling down into
29 smaller skills involving letters and sounds, and then builds on
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
263
1 these skills over time.
2 (e) The programming uses multisensory teaching strategies to
3 teach reading by using sight, hearing, touch, and movement to help
4 students connect and learn the concepts being taught.
5 (3) Districts and intermediate districts may apply to the
6 department for grants to purchase components or training through an
7 eligible teacher training program from a provider of an eligible
8 teacher training program, and, upon receiving an application and
9 except as otherwise provided in this subsection, the department
10 shall make payments to districts and intermediate districts for
11 those purchases. The department shall make payments under this
12 section on a first-come, first-served basis until funds are
13 depleted.
14 Sec. 35m. (1) From the state school aid fund money

18 improve educational outcomes in literacy.
19 (2) The department shall continue the committee for literacy
20 achievement that includes representatives of higher education and
21 early childhood literacy educators who have expertise in literacy
22 instruction and research. The committee shall exist until at least
23 September 2029. The department shall appoint the members of the
24 committee for literacy achievement. The department shall develop a
25 process to identify and assemble experts for the committee. The
26 primary focus of the committee is to recommend tools and strategies
27 with the goal of increasing student academic outcomes in the area
28 of literacy. The committee is encouraged to collaborate with the
29 Michigan Education Research Institute or other entities with
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
264
1 similar expertise on the subject of literacy. The department shall
2 determine the size and benchmarks of the committee. The committee
3 shall do all of the following:
4 (a) Critically evaluate early literacy series and materials.
5 The committee shall evaluate with research-based outcomes, using
6 the committee's expertise in the field of literacy. The committee
7 shall assign grade tiers to the series based on the series'
8 likelihood to increase student literacy outcomes. Priority must be
9 given to series that already have a demonstrated history of
10 increasing student outcomes. The committee may perform an
11 evaluation under this subdivision based on submissions from a
12 vendor, but must independently verify the validity of information
13 provided by the vendor. The committee is also strongly encouraged
14 to evaluate available materials not submitted to the department by
15 a vendor.
16 (b) Critically evaluate literacy professional development. The
17 committee shall evaluate with research-based outcomes, using the
18 committee's expertise in the field of literacy. The committee shall
19 assign grade tiers to the professional development based on the
20 professional development's likelihood to increase student literacy
21 outcomes. Priority must be given to professional development that
22 already has a demonstrated history of increasing student outcomes.
23 The committee may perform an evaluation under this subdivision
24 based on submissions from a vendor, but must independently verify
25 the validity of information provided by the vendor. The committee
26 is also strongly encouraged to evaluate available materials not
27 submitted to the department by a vendor.
28 (c) Critically evaluate other applicable literacy tools or
29 services the committee determines to have a high likelihood or
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
265
1 demonstrated history of increasing student literacy outcomes. The
2 committee may perform an evaluation under this subdivision based on
3 submissions from a vendor, but must independently verify the
4 validity of information provided by the vendor. The committee is
5 also strongly encouraged to evaluate available materials not
6 submitted to the department by a vendor.
7 (d) Create, maintain, and post annually on a publicly
8 available website a rankings list of all early literacy series and
9 other items the committee has evaluated. This includes items from
10 each part of the rankings list, items evaluated in the 2024-2025
11 school year, and any subsequent items evaluated going forward. The
12 committee shall denote on the list whether major changes have been
13 made to an early a literacy series or other item on the list since
14 the early literacy series or other item was evaluated by the
15 committee and if the early literacy series or other item has not
16 been evaluated since the last ranking. The rankings list created in
17 this subdivision must align with the list of evidence-based tier 1,
18 classwide elementary reading curricula and materials that are
19 aligned with science of reading methods that research has shown to
20 improve literacy outcomes and help pupils achieve reading
21 proficiency as required under section 1280f of the revised school
22 code, MCL 380.1280f. A list described in this subdivision may
23 include a maximum of 15 recommendations. Additional items may be
24 evaluated. However, only the top 15 may be recommended for use.
25 (3) An individual on the committee shall recuse themselves
26 from evaluating early literacy series and other items that the
27 individual has helped create, that the individual would benefit
28 from financially, or for which the individual has any other
29 conflict of interest.

1 (4) From the funds allocated in subsection (1), the department

11 funding is allocated in subsection (1) to districts and
12 intermediate districts. Districts and intermediate districts using
13 higher-ranked literacy tools that are proven to increase student
14 outcomes shall receive more funding than districts and intermediate
15 districts utilizing lower-ranked literacy tools in both the base
16 award and the per-pupil awards described in this subsection. The
17 department may determine that some districts and intermediate
18 districts will not receive funding under this section based on the
19 effectiveness of the early literacy series or other items being
20 utilized by the district or intermediate district. The department
21 shall award funding under this section using the following
22 methodology:
23 (a) A base award equal to the amount necessary for the
24 district or intermediate district to implement the chosen early
25 literacy series or other item. The department may place a cap on
26 the total award per district, intermediate district, or early
27 literacy series or other item and may choose not to award funding
28 if the early literacy series or other item is determined to be
29 ineffective or is determined to be less effective than other
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
267
1 choices.
2 (b) A tiered per-pupil award based on the number of pupils in
3 membership at the district and intermediate district. The tiered
4 per-pupil award must provide a higher per-pupil payment to
5 districts and intermediate districts using higher-ranked early
6 literacy series or other items as determined under subsection (2).
7 The department may choose not to award funding if the early
8 literacy series or other item is determined to be ineffective or is
9 determined to be less effective than other choices.

1 following:

4 (iii) Provides intensive support with a minimum of 2 sessions
5 per week.
6 (iv) Uses a structured, evidence-based curriculum designed for
7 tutoring, with formative assessments to track progress.
8 (v) Uses trained tutors.
9 (vi) Includes ongoing assessment and feedback.

8 Sec. 36. (1) From the state school aid fund money appropriated
9 in section 11, there is allocated for 2026-2027 only an amount not

12 (2) The department shall create the committee for mathematics
13 achievement, which must include representatives of higher education
14 and mathematics educators who have expertise in mathematics
15 instruction and research. The committee shall exist until at least
16 September 2029. The department shall appoint the members of the
17 committee for mathematics achievement. The department shall develop
18 a process to identify and assemble experts for the committee for
19 mathematics achievement. The primary focus of the committee for
20 mathematics achievement is to recommend curricula and materials
21 with the goal of increasing student academic outcomes in the area
22 of mathematics. The committee for mathematics achievement is
23 encouraged to collaborate with entities with expertise on the
24 subject of mathematics. The department shall determine the size and
25 benchmarks of the committee for mathematics achievement. The
26 committee for mathematics achievement shall do all of the
27 following:
28 (a) Critically evaluate K to 5 mathematics curricula and
29 materials. The committee for mathematics achievement shall evaluate
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
281
1 with research-based outcomes, using the committee for mathematics
2 achievement's expertise in the field of mathematics. The committee
3 for mathematics achievement shall assign grade tiers to a
4 curriculum based on the curriculum's likelihood of increasing
5 student mathematics outcomes. Priority must be given to curricula
6 that already have a demonstrated history of increasing student
7 outcomes. The committee for mathematics achievement may perform an
8 evaluation under this subdivision based on submissions from a
9 vendor, but must independently verify the validity of information
10 provided by the vendor. The committee for mathematics achievement
11 is also strongly encouraged to evaluate available materials not
12 submitted to the department by a vendor.
13 (b) Create, maintain, and annually post on a publicly
14 available website a rankings list of all K to 5 mathematics
15 curricula the committee for mathematics achievement has evaluated.
16 This includes items from each part of the rankings list, items
17 evaluated in the 2026-2027 school year, and any subsequent items
18 evaluated going forward. The committee for mathematics achievement
19 shall denote on the list whether major changes have been made to a
20 mathematics curriculum on the list since the mathematics curriculum
21 was evaluated by the committee for mathematics achievement and if
22 the mathematics curriculum has not been evaluated since the last
23 ranking.
24 (c) The committee for mathematics achievement must select not
25 more than 5 mathematics curricula to recommend for purchase by
26 districts.
27 (3) An individual on the committee for mathematics achievement
28 shall recuse themselves from evaluating mathematics curricula or
29 other items that the individual has helped create, that the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
282
1 individual would benefit from financially, or for which the
2 individual has any other conflict of interest.

24 purchased using funds under section 36.

24 option who meet the age-eligible criteria of section 32d.

5 Sec. 51i. (1) It is the intent of the legislature that,
6 beginning in the fiscal year ending September 30, 2028, a weighted
7 special education funding formula begins to be implemented to
8 recognize the higher costs associated with certain student needs.
9 (2) It is the intent of the legislature that a weighted
10 special education funding formula described in subsection (1) be
11 implemented in a way to work toward the following target weights
12 for payments to districts and intermediate districts for identified
13 special education students:
14 (a) Speech and language impairment, 1.1.
15 (b) Specific learning disability, 1.1.
16 (c) Other health impairment multiplied, 1.8.
17 (d) Physical impairment multiplied, 3.1.
18 (e) Severe multiple impairments, 3.1.
19 (f) Cognitive impairment, 3.1.
20 (g) Deaf or hard of hearing multiplied, 3.1.
21 (h) Deaf-blindness, 3.1.
22 (i) Emotional impairment multiplied, 3.8.
23 (j) Visual impairment, 3.8.
24 (k) Early childhood developmental delays, 3.8.
25 (l) Autism spectrum disorder, 3.8.
26 (m) Traumatic brain injury, 3.8.
27 (3) It is the intent of the legislature that payments to
28 districts and intermediate districts under the formula described in
29 this section be adjusted to take into account available federal

28 openings and median wage for each standard occupational code in
29 each career cluster as obtained from the United States Bureau of
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
321
1 Labor Statistics. Standard occupational codes within high-ranking
2 clusters also may be further ranked by median wage and annual job
3 openings. The career and educational advisory council located in
4 the workforce development board service delivery area shall review
5 the rankings and modify them if necessary to accurately reflect
6 employer demand for talent in the workforce development board
7 service delivery area. A career and educational advisory council
8 shall document that it has conducted this review and certify that
9 it is accurate. These career cluster rankings must be determined
10 and updated once every 4 years.
11 (b) An identification of educational entities in the workforce
12 development board service delivery area that will provide eligible
13 CTE early middle college and CTE dual enrollment programs including
14 districts, intermediate districts, postsecondary institutions, and
15 noncredit occupational training programs leading to an industry-
16 recognized credential.
17 (c) A strategy to inform parents and students of CTE early
18 middle college and CTE dual enrollment programs in the workforce
19 development board service delivery area.
20 (d) Any other requirements as defined by the department.
21 (5) An eligible CTE program is a program that meets all of the
22 following:
23 (a) Has been identified in the highest 5 career cluster
24 rankings in any of the 16 workforce development board service
25 delivery area strategic plans jointly approved by the department of
26 labor and economic opportunity and the department.
27 (b) Has a coherent sequence of courses in a specific career
28 cluster that will allow a student to earn a high school diploma and
29 achieve at least 1 of the following:
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
322
1 (i) For CTE early middle college, outcomes as defined in
2 subsection (3)(f).
3 (ii) For CTE dual enrollment, 1 of the following:
4 (A) An associate degree.
5 (B) An industry-recognized technical certification approved by
6 the department of labor and economic opportunity.
7 (C) Up to 60 transferable college credits.
8 (D) Participation in a registered apprenticeship, pre-
9 apprenticeship, or apprentice readiness program.
10 (c) Is aligned with the Michigan merit curriculum.
11 (d) Has an articulation or a college credit agreement with at
12 least 1 postsecondary institution that provides students with
13 opportunities to receive postsecondary credits during the student's
14 participation in the CTE early middle college or CTE dual
15 enrollment program and transfers those credits to the postsecondary
16 institution upon completion of the CTE early middle college or CTE
17 dual enrollment program.
18 (e) Provides instruction that is supervised, directed, or
19 coordinated by an appropriately certificated CTE teacher or, for
20 concurrent enrollment courses, a postsecondary faculty member.
21 (f) Provides for highly integrated student support services
22 that include at least the following:
23 (i) Teachers as academic advisors.
24 (ii) Supervised course selection.
25 (iii) Monitoring of student progress and completion.
26 (iv) Career planning services provided by a local one-stop
27 service center as described in the Michigan works one-stop service
28 center system act, 2006 PA 491, MCL 408.111 to 408.135, or by a
29 high school counselor or advisor.
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
323
1 (g) Has courses that are taught on a college campus, are
2 college courses offered at the high school and taught by college
3 faculty, or are courses taught in combination with online
4 instruction.
5 (6) The department shall distribute funds to eligible CTE
6 early middle college and CTE dual enrollment programs as follows:
7 (a) The department shall determine statewide average CTE costs
8 per pupil for each CIP code program by calculating statewide
9 average costs for each CIP code program for the 3 most recent
10 fiscal years.
11 (b) The distribution to each eligible CTE early middle college
12 or CTE dual enrollment program is the product of 50% of CTE costs
13 per pupil times the pupil enrollment of each eligible CTE early
14 middle college or CTE dual enrollment program in the immediately
15 preceding school year.
16 (7) To receive funds under this section, a CTE early middle
17 college or CTE dual enrollment program shall furnish to the
18 intermediate district that is the fiscal agent identified in
19 subsection (2), in a form and manner determined by the department,
20 all information needed to administer this program and meet federal
21 reporting requirements; shall allow the department or the
22 department's designee to review all records related to the program
23 for which it receives funds; and shall reimburse the state for all
24 disallowances found in the review, as determined by the department.
25 (8) There is allocated for 2025-2026 2026-2027 from the funds

11 code, MCL 380.1278a and 380.1278b.

22 Sec. 94a. (1) There is created within the state budget office
23 in the department of technology, management, and budget the center
24 for educational performance and information. The center shall do
25 all of the following:
26 (a) Coordinate the collection of all data required by state
27 and federal law from districts, intermediate districts, and
28 postsecondary institutions.
29 (b) Create, maintain, and enhance this state's P-20
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
342
1 longitudinal data system and ensure that it meets the requirements
2 of subsection (4).
3 (c) Collect data in the most efficient manner possible to
4 reduce the administrative burden on reporting entities, including,
5 but not limited to, electronic transcript services.
6 (d) Create, maintain, and enhance this state's web-based
7 educational portal to provide information to school leaders,
8 teachers, researchers, and the public in compliance with all
9 federal and state privacy laws. Data must include, but are not
10 limited to, all of the following:
11 (i) Data sets that link teachers to student information,
12 allowing districts to assess individual teacher impact on student
13 performance and consider student growth factors in teacher and
14 principal evaluation systems.
15 (ii) Data access or, if practical, data sets, provided for
16 regional data hubs that, in combination with local data, can
17 improve teaching and learning in the classroom.
18 (iii) Research-ready data sets for researchers to perform
19 research that advances this state's educational performance.
20 (e) Provide data in a useful manner to allow state and local
21 policymakers to make informed policy decisions.
22 (f) Provide public reports to the residents of this state to
23 allow them to assess allocation of resources and the return on
24 their investment in the education system of this state.
25 (g) Other functions as assigned by the state budget director.
26 (2) Each state department, officer, or agency that collects
27 information from districts, intermediate districts, or
28 postsecondary institutions as required under state or federal law
29 shall make arrangements with the center to ensure that the state
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
343
1 department, officer, or agency is in compliance with subsection
2 (1). This subsection does not apply to information collected by the
3 department of treasury under the uniform budgeting and accounting
4 act, 1968 PA 2, MCL 141.421 to 141.440a; the revised municipal
5 finance act, 2001 PA 34, MCL 141.2101 to 141.2821; the school bond
6 qualification, approval, and loan act, 2005 PA 92, MCL 388.1921 to
7 388.1939; or section 1351a of the revised school code, MCL
8 380.1351a.
9 (3) The center may enter into any interlocal agreements
10 necessary to fulfill its functions.
11 (4) The center shall ensure that the P-20 longitudinal data
12 system required under subsection (1)(b) meets all of the following:
13 (a) Includes data at the individual student level from
14 preschool through postsecondary education and into the workforce.
15 (b) Supports interoperability by using standard data
16 structures, data formats, and data definitions to ensure linkage
17 and connectivity in a manner that facilitates the exchange of data
18 among agencies and institutions within the state and between
19 states.
20 (c) Enables the matching of individual teacher and student
21 records so that an individual student may be matched with those
22 teachers providing instruction to that student.
23 (d) Enables the matching of individual teachers with
24 information about their certification and the institutions that
25 prepared and recommended those teachers for state certification.
26 (e) Enables data to be easily generated for continuous
27 improvement and decision-making, including timely reporting to
28 parents, teachers, and school leaders on student achievement.
29 (f) Ensures the reasonable quality, validity, and reliability
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
344
1 of data contained in the system.
2 (g) Provides this state with the ability to meet federal and
3 state reporting requirements.
4 (h) For data elements related to preschool through grade 12
5 and postsecondary, meets all of the following:
6 (i) Contains a unique statewide student identifier that does
7 not permit a student to be individually identified by users of the
8 system, except as allowed by federal and state law.
9 (ii) Contains student-level enrollment, demographic, and
10 program participation information, including data associated with
11 students who have been identified as having an affiliation to 1 or
12 more federally recognized Indian tribes and student participation
13 in federal programs funded under 20 USC 7401 to 7546 and

15 Supplemental supplemental Indian Education Program Modernization
16 Act, education program modernization act, Public Law 115-404. Any

18 must be done through ongoing consultation with the federally
19 recognized tribes in the state with the expectation that the
20 center, the department, and the tribes will work iteratively toward
21 meaningful reports, access, and use of these records to improve
22 shared education interests and outcomes.
23 (iii) Contains student-level information about the points at
24 which students exit, transfer in, transfer out, drop out, or
25 complete education programs.
26 (iv) Has the capacity to communicate with higher education data
27 systems.
28 (i) For data elements related to preschool through grade 12
29 only, meets all of the following:
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
345
1 (i) Contains yearly test records of individual students for
2 assessments approved by DED-OESE for accountability purposes under
3 section 1111(b) of the elementary and secondary education act of
4 1965, 20 USC 6311, including information on individual students not
5 tested, by grade and subject.
6 (ii) Contains student-level transcript information, including
7 information on courses completed and grades earned.

13 (2) The Michigan Virtual University shall operate the Michigan
14 Virtual Learning Research Institute. The Michigan Virtual Learning

16 (a) Support and accelerate innovation in education through the

22 multimedia instructional content.

24 department criteria by which cyber schools and virtual course

26 education for their pupils.

352
1 of virtual learning delivery models in preparing pupils to be

6 subcommittees on school aid, the state budget director, the house
7 and senate fiscal agencies, the department, districts, and
8 intermediate districts by not later than March 31 of each fiscal
9 year for which funding is allocated under this section.

11 at least 30,000 educational personnel, including teachers, school
12 administrators, and school board members, that focuses on the
13 effective integration of virtual learning into curricula and
14 instruction. The Michigan Virtual Learning Research Institute is

18 Institute and external stakeholders are encouraged to coordinate

1 virtual educational services to districts and a summary of the

11 (12) As used in this section:
12 (a) "Blended learning" means a hybrid instructional delivery
13 model where pupils are provided content, instruction, and
14 assessment, in part at a supervised educational facility away from
15 home where the pupil and a teacher with a valid Michigan teaching
16 certificate are in the same physical location and in part through
17 internet-connected learning environments with some degree of pupil
18 control over time, location, and pace of instruction.
19 (b) "Cyber school" means a full-time instructional program of
20 virtual courses for pupils that may or may not require attendance
21 at a physical school location.
22 (c) "Virtual course" means a course of study that is capable
23 of generating a credit or a grade and that is provided in an
24 interactive learning environment in which the majority of the
25 curriculum is delivered using the internet and in which pupils are
26 separated from their instructor or teacher of record by time or
27 location, or both.

1 this section must do all of the following:

8 Sec. 101. (1) To be eligible to receive state aid under this
9 article, not later than the fifth Wednesday after the pupil

13 superintendent, in the form and manner prescribed by the center,

15 including identification of tuition-paying pupils, in the district
16 as of the pupil membership count day and as of the supplemental
17 count day, as applicable, for the current school year. In addition,
18 a district maintaining school during the entire year shall submit
19 and certify to the center and the intermediate superintendent, in
20 the form and manner prescribed by the center, the number of pupils
21 enrolled and in regular daily attendance in the district for the
22 current school year pursuant to rules promulgated by the
23 superintendent. Not later than the sixth Wednesday after the pupil
24 membership count day and not later than the sixth Wednesday after
25 the supplemental count day, the district shall resolve any pupil
26 membership conflicts with another district, correct any data
27 issues, and recertify the data in a form and manner prescribed by
28 the center and file the certified data with the intermediate
29 superintendent. If a district fails to submit and certify the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
373
1 attendance data, as required under this subsection, the center
2 shall notify the department and the department shall withhold state
3 aid due to be distributed under this article from the defaulting
4 district immediately, beginning with the next payment after the
5 failure and continuing with each payment until the district
6 complies with this subsection. If a district does not comply with
7 this subsection by the end of the fiscal year, the district
8 forfeits the amount withheld. A person who willfully falsifies a
9 figure or statement in the certified and sworn copy of enrollment
10 is subject to penalty as prescribed by section 161.

1 conferences are not counted as hours or days of pupil instruction.

3 this subsection, if a district does not have at least 75% of the

9 the end of its instructional calendar for a school year to comply
10 with subdivision (a) because the district otherwise would fail to

24 (10) A district may count up to 38 hours of professional

26 following apply to the counting of professional development as
27 pupil instruction under this subsection:
28 (a) If the professional development exceeds 5 hours in a
29 single day, that day may be counted as a day of pupil instruction.
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
386
1 (b) At least 8 hours of the professional development counted
2 as hours of pupil instruction under this subsection must be
3 recommended by a districtwide professional development advisory
4 committee appointed by the district board. The advisory committee
5 must be composed of teachers employed by the district who represent
6 a variety of grades and subject matter specializations, including
7 special education; nonteaching staff; parents; and administrators.
8 The majority membership of the committee must be composed of
9 teaching staff.
10 (c) Professional development provided online is allowable and
11 encouraged, as long as the instruction has been approved by the
12 district. The department shall issue a list of approved online
13 professional development providers that must include the Michigan
14 Virtual School.
15 (d) Professional development may only be counted as hours of
16 pupil instruction under this subsection for the pupils of those
17 teachers scheduled to participate in the professional development.
18 (e) The professional development must meet all of the
19 following to be counted as pupil instruction under this subsection:
20 (i) Be aligned to the school or district improvement plan for
21 the school or district in which the professional development is
22 being provided.
23 (ii) Be linked to 1 or more criteria in the evaluation tool
24 developed or adopted by the district or intermediate district under
25 section 1249 of the revised school code, MCL 380.1249.
26 (iii) Has been approved by the department as counting for state
27 continuing education clock hours. The number of hours of
28 professional development counted as hours of pupil instruction
29 under this subsection may not exceed the number of state continuing
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
387
1 education clock hours for which the professional development was
2 approved.
3 (iv) Not more than a combined total of 10 hours of the
4 professional development takes place before the first scheduled day
5 of school for the school year ending in the fiscal year and after
6 the last scheduled day of school for that school year.
7 (v) Not more than 10 hours of the professional development
8 takes place in a single month.
9 (vi) At least 75% of teachers scheduled to participate in the
10 professional development are in attendance.

15 section shall not use those funds for any other purpose.
16 (2) To be eligible for funding under this section, an eligible
17 adult education provider shall employ certificated teachers and
18 qualified administrative staff and shall offer continuing education
19 opportunities for teachers to allow them to maintain certification.
20 (3) To be eligible to be a participant funded under this
21 section, an individual must be enrolled in an adult basic education
22 program, an adult secondary education program, an adult English as
23 a second language program, a high school equivalency test
24 preparation program, or a high school completion program, that
25 meets the requirements of this section, and for which instruction
26 is provided, and the individual must be at least 18 years of age by
27 July 1 of the program year and the individual's graduating class
28 must have graduated.

395
1 allocated under this section, the intermediate districts within a

6 department shall approve or disapprove of the prosperity region's
7 or subregion's selected fiscal agent. From the funds allocated

11 regions or subregions identified by the department. An intermediate
12 district shall not use more than 5% of the funds allocated under

20 subregions located within the intermediate district, as reported by
21 the most recent 5-year estimates from the American Community Survey
22 (ACS) from the United States Census Bureau.

25 individuals age 25 or older who are not high school graduates that
26 resides in each of the prosperity regions or subregions located
27 within the intermediate district, as reported by the most recent 5-
28 year estimates from the ACS from the United States Census Bureau.

396
1 distributed based upon the proportion of the state population of

3 proficiency that resides in each of the prosperity regions or

8 must agree to do the following in a form and manner determined by
9 the department:
10 (a) Distribute funds to adult education programs in a
11 prosperity region or subregion as described in this section.
12 (b) Collaborate with the career and educational advisory
13 council, which is an advisory council of the workforce development

15 successor, to develop a regional strategy that aligns adult
16 education programs and services into an efficient and effective
17 delivery system for adult education learners, with special
18 consideration for providing contextualized learning and career
19 pathways and addressing barriers to education and employment.
20 (c) Collaborate with the career and educational advisory
21 council, which is an advisory council of the workforce development
22 boards located in the prosperity region or subregion, or its
23 successor, to create a local process and criteria that will
24 identify eligible adult education providers to receive funds
25 allocated under this section based on location, demand for
26 services, past performance, quality indicators as identified by the
27 department, and cost to provide instructional services. The fiscal
28 agent shall determine all local processes, criteria, and provider
29 determinations. However, the local processes, criteria, and
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
397
1 provider services must be approved by the department before funds
2 may be distributed to the fiscal agent.
3 (d) Provide oversight to its adult education providers
4 throughout the program year to ensure compliance with the
5 requirements of this section.
6 (e) Report adult education program and participant data and
7 information as prescribed by the department.
8 (6) An adult basic education program, an adult secondary
9 education program, or an adult English as a second language program
10 operated on a year-round or school year basis may be funded under
11 this section, subject to all of the following:
12 (a) The program enrolls adults who are determined by a
13 department-approved assessment, in a form and manner prescribed by
14 the department, to be below twelfth grade level in reading or
15 mathematics, or both, or to lack basic English proficiency.
16 (b) The program tests individuals for eligibility under
17 subdivision (a) before enrollment and upon completion of the
18 program in compliance with the state-approved assessment policy.
19 (c) A participant in an adult basic education program is
20 eligible for reimbursement until 1 of the following occurs:
21 (i) The participant's reading and mathematics proficiency are
22 assessed at or above the ninth grade level.
23 (ii) The participant fails to show progress on 2 successive
24 assessments after having completed at least 450 hours of
25 instruction.
26 (d) A participant in an adult secondary education program is
27 eligible for reimbursement until 1 of the following occurs:
28 (i) The participant's reading and mathematics proficiency are
29 assessed above the twelfth grade level.
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
398
1 (ii) The participant fails to show progress on 2 successive
2 assessments after having at least 450 hours of instruction.
3 (e) A funding recipient enrolling a participant in an English
4 as a second language program is eligible for funding according to
5 subsection (9) until the participant meets 1 of the following:
6 (i) The participant is assessed as having attained basic
7 English proficiency as determined by a department-approved
8 assessment.
9 (ii) The participant fails to show progress on 2 successive
10 department-approved assessments after having completed at least 450
11 hours of instruction. The department shall provide information to a
12 funding recipient regarding appropriate assessment instruments for
13 this program.
14 (7) A high school equivalency test preparation program
15 operated on a year-round or school year basis may be funded under
16 this section, subject to all of the following:
17 (a) The program enrolls adults who do not have a high school
18 diploma or a high school equivalency certificate.
19 (b) The program administers a pre-test approved by the
20 department before enrolling an individual to determine the
21 individual's literacy levels, administers a high school equivalency
22 practice test to determine the individual's potential for success
23 on the high school equivalency test, and administers a post-test
24 upon completion of the program in compliance with the state-
25 approved assessment policy.
26 (c) A funding recipient receives funding according to
27 subsection (9) for a participant, and a participant may be enrolled
28 in the program until 1 of the following occurs:
29 (i) The participant achieves a high school equivalency
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
399
1 certificate.
2 (ii) The participant fails to show progress on 2 successive
3 department-approved assessments used to determine readiness to take

5 hours of instruction.

4 (b) "Career pathway" means a combination of rigorous and high-

6 all of the following:

17 with and in the same context as workforce preparation activities
18 and training for a specific occupation or occupational cluster.

20 the particular needs of an individual in a manner that accelerates
21 the educational and career advancement of the individual to the
22 extent practicable.
23 (vi) Enables an individual to attain a secondary school diploma
24 or its recognized equivalent, and at least 1 recognized
25 postsecondary credential.
26 (vii) Helps an individual enter or advance within a specific
27 occupation or occupational cluster.

9 safety, or welfare requirement under a law or administrative rule
10 of this state. The superintendent shall calculate a nonpublic

19 wage of its lowest-paid employee capable of performing a specific
20 task regardless of whether that individual is available and
21 regardless of who actually performs a specific task. Labor costs
22 under this subsection must be estimated and charged in increments
23 of 15 minutes or more, with all partial time increments rounded
24 down. When calculating costs under subsection (4), fee components
25 must be itemized in a manner that expresses both the hourly wage
26 and the number of hours charged. The nonpublic school may not
27 charge any applicable labor charge amount to cover or partially
28 cover the cost of health or fringe benefits. A nonpublic school
29 shall not charge any overtime wages in the calculation of labor

3 to, student achievement and success, literacy, mathematics, and
4 providing direct English language development instruction.
5 (b) Administer to English language learners the English
6 language proficiency assessment known as the "WIDA ACCESS for
7 English language learners" or the "WIDA Alternate ACCESS".
8 (c) Agree to meet or exceed the minimum number of minutes per
9 week, as determined by the department under subsection (2), that
10 the district provides direct English language development
11 instruction.
12 (d) Implement a multi-tiered system of supports that is an
13 evidence-based framework that uses data-driven problem solving to
14 integrate academic and behavioral instruction and that uses
15 intervention delivered to all pupils in varying intensities based
16 on pupil needs. The multi-tiered system of supports must be
17 implemented in grades K to 12 or, if the district does not operate
18 all of grades K to 12, for all of the grades the district operates.
19 The multi-tiered system of supports described in this subdivision
20 must provide at least all of the following essential components:
21 (i) Team-based leadership.
22 (ii) A tiered delivery system.
23 (iii) Selection and implementation of instruction,
24 interventions, and supports.
25 (iv) A comprehensive screening and assessment system.
26 (v) Continuous data-based decision making.
27 (e) Support pupil needs for pupils counted toward the
28 district's weighted pupil membership, including, but not limited
29 to, items identified in a district's Michigan Integrated Continuous
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
435
1 Improvement Process (MICIP) plan. An amount not to exceed 2% of the
2 amount received under section 22b(4) may be used to support
3 administrative costs related to complying with this section.
4 Administrative costs include, but are not limited to, central
5 office salaries and benefits, business and fiscal services, and
6 school administrative staff and expenses, and do not include costs
7 associated with direct instructional staff working with pupils
8 counted toward the weighted pupil membership.
9 (f) Provide information to the department, in a form and
10 manner determined by the department, necessary for the department
11 to monitor programs and services described in this section for
12 compliance with the requirements of this section and to ensure the
13 multi-tiered system of supports and English language learner
14 programs are implemented with fidelity.
15 (g) Allow access for the department or the department's
16 designee to audit all records related to the program for which the
17 district receives those funds. The district shall reimburse this
18 state for all disallowances found in any audit.
19 (h) Communicate the following information to parents and legal
20 guardians at least annually, as applicable for grades offered by
21 the district:
22 (i) Literacy-related results for the district, including
23 multiyear trend data, from the grade 3 Michigan student test of
24 educational progress (M-STEP), the grade 7 Michigan student test of
25 educational progress (M-STEP), and the SAT for pupils who are
26 economically disadvantaged or English language learners. The
27 district shall ensure that information described in this
28 subparagraph is reported in a manner that safeguards pupil privacy.
29 (ii) Information detailing the amount of funding received under
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
436
1 section 22b(4), how the district distributed that funding, and what
2 evidence-based interventions were implemented with that funding.
3 The information reported under this subparagraph must include a
4 method, including contact information, for parents or legal
5 guardians to provide feedback on the use of the funding as well as
6 to seek more information about services and interventions available
7 for their children.
8 (2) The department shall establish English language learner
9 program models that establish a minimum number of minutes per week
10 that districts must provide direct English language development
11 instruction for pupils according to the pupil's proficiency levels.
12 The program models described in this subsection must comply with
13 federal requirements related to English language learner program
14 services.
15 (3) The department shall monitor districts for compliance with
16 this section and may require districts to provide any information
17 to the department, in a form and manner determined by the
18 department, necessary to fulfill this requirement.
19 (4) As used in this section, "English language learner" and
20 "weighted pupil membership" mean those terms as defined in section
21 6.
22 Sec. 164n. By not later than 6 months after the state budget
23 office issues work project letters, and again on or before April
24 15, the department and the department of lifelong education,
25 advancement, and potential shall submit an annual report to the
26 senate and house appropriations subcommittees on school aid, the
27 senate and house fiscal agencies, and the senate and house policy
28 offices that summarizes all work project accounts. The report
29 described in this section must include all of the following:
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
437
1 (a) A list of all work project accounts.
2 (b) The status of all work project accounts, including amounts
3 expended, amounts encumbered, and available balances for each
4 account.
5 (c) The amount of funds that lapsed from any previously
6 designated work project accounts, the name and description of the
7 work project account, and the funds that received the lapsed
8 amounts.
9 Sec. 164o. Districts and intermediate districts are encouraged
10 to evaluate and pursue efficiency and cost-containment measures
11 that maximize state funding. Districts and intermediate districts
12 shall identify practices that increase efficiencies, including, but
13 not limited to, establishing joint ventures, consolidating
14 services, utilizing program collaborations, maximizing educational
15 benefits through optimal class sizes and frequency of course
16 offerings, eliminating low-enrollment and high-cost instructional
17 programs, using self-insurance, practicing energy conservation, and
18 utilizing group purchasing.

4 Sec. 201i. (1) Not later than Within 30 days after of the
5 enactment of the an amendatory act that added this section, to this
6 article, the legislature house and senate shall provide to the
7 responsible entity and the state budget director office a jointly
8 agreed upon list of legislatively directed spending items , which
9 may be referred to in this section as grants or direct
10 appropriation grants, funded under the amendatory act that added
11 this section consistent with house or senate rules and this
12 section. funded by the amendatory act. The list must include all
13 information and documents pertaining to the funded items as
14 publicly disclosed in accordance with house or senate rules and
15 this section. sections 364 and 364a of the management and budget
16 act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and 18.1364a. As used in this
17 subsection, "legislatively directed spending item" means that term
18 as defined in section 364 of the management and budget act, 1984 PA
19 431, MCL 18.1364.
20 (2) Notwithstanding any other conditions or requirements for
21 direct appropriation grants, the responsible entity shall perform,
22 at a minimum, at least all of the following activities to
23 administer the grants described in subsection (1):
24 (a) Establish a process to review, complete, and execute a
25 grant agreement with a grant recipient. The responsible entity
26 shall not execute a grant agreement unless all necessary
27 documentation has been submitted and reviewed.
28 (b) Verify to the extent possible that a grant recipient is a
29 not-for-profit entity and will use funds as publicly disclosed and
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
448
1 for a public purpose that serves the economic prosperity, health,
2 safety, or general welfare of the residents of this state.
3 (c) Review and verify all necessary information to ensure the
4 grant recipient is reasonably able to execute the grant agreement,
5 perform its fiduciary duty, and comply with all applicable state
6 and federal statutes. The responsible entity may deduct the cost of
7 background checks and any other efforts performed as part of this
8 verification from the amount of the designated grant award.
9 (d) Disburse the grant money per the grant disbursement
10 schedule in the executed grant agreement on a reimbursement basis
11 after the grantee has provided sufficient documentation, as
12 determined by the responsible entity, to verify that expenditures
13 were made in accordance with the project purpose.
14 (e) If the state budget director determines that information
15 provided by the grantee does not meet the disclosure requirements,
16 that the grant will be used to pay a tax lien, delinquent tax, or
17 other obligation owed to this state, or that the grant will create
18 a conflict of interest, the responsible entity shall not release
19 the grant money to the grantee. Money that is not released under
20 this subdivision lapses at the end of the fiscal year. There is not
21 a conflict of interest if the sponsoring legislator certifies that
22 the sponsoring legislator's immediate family members, legislative
23 staff members that have worked for the sponsoring legislator within
24 the past 2 years, and the sponsoring legislator do not have a
25 direct or indirect pecuniary interest in the legislatively directed
26 spending item.
27 (3) An executed grant agreement under this section between the
28 responsible entity and a grant recipient must include at least all
29 of the following:
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
449
1 (a) All necessary identifying information for the grant
2 recipient, including any tax and financial information necessary
3 for the responsible entity to administer grant money under this
4 section.
5 (b) A description of the project for which the grant money
6 will be expended, including tentative timelines and the estimated
7 budget. Project budget must include how all grant money will be
8 used and must indicate if any grant money will be provided to a
9 third party or subrecipient. The responsible entity shall not
10 reimburse expenditures that are outside of the project purpose, as
11 stated in the executed grant agreement, from appropriations under
12 the amendatory act that added this section. The grantee shall
13 return to the state treasury any interest in excess of $1,000.00
14 earned on the grant money while unexpended and in possession of the
15 grantee.
16 (c) Unless otherwise specified in the responsible entity's
17 policy, a requirement that funds appropriated for the grants
18 described in subsection (1) may be used only for expenditures that
19 occur on or after the effective date of the amendatory act that
20 added this section.
21 (d) A requirement for reporting by the grant recipient to the
22 responsible entity and the legislative sponsor that provides the
23 status of the project and an accounting of all money expended by
24 the grant recipient, as determined by the responsible entity.
25 (e) A clawback provision that allows the department of
26 treasury to recoup or otherwise collect any grant money that is
27 declined, unspent, or otherwise misused.
28 (f) The documents publicly disclosed under subsection (1).
29 (4) If appropriate to improve the administration or oversight

1 of a grant described in subsection (1), the responsible entity may
2 adopt a memorandum of understanding with another state department
3 to perform the required duties under this section.
4 (5) A grant recipient shall respond to all reasonable
5 information requests from the responsible entity related to grant
6 expenditures and retain grant records for not less than 7 years,
7 and the grant may be subject to monitoring, site visits, and audit
8 as determined by the responsible entity. The grant agreement
9 required under this section must include signed assurance by the

12 requirements of this subsection will be met.
13 (6) The grant recipient shall expend all grant money awarded

16 return that money to the state treasury. If a grant recipient does
17 not provide information sufficient to execute a grant agreement not
18 later than June 1, 2026, the responsible entity shall return money
19 associated with the grant to the state treasury.
20 (7) Any grant money that is awarded to a responsible entity is
21 appropriated in that responsible entity for the purpose of the
22 intended grant.

3 Sec. 202a. As used in this article:
4 (a) "Center" means the center for educational performance and
5 information created in section 94a.
6 (b) "College level equivalent credit examination" means an
7 examination that is administered by an independent testing service
8 and that is used by colleges and universities generally to award
9 postsecondary credit for achievement of a particular score, and
10 includes, but is not limited to, advanced placement examinations,
11 the DANTES Subject Standardized Test (DSST), and college-level
12 examination program (CLEP) examinations.
13 (c) "Participating college" means a community college that is
14 a reporting unit of the retirement system and that reports
15 employees to the retirement system for the state fiscal year.
16 (d) "Retirement system" means the Michigan public school
17 employees' retirement system under the public school employees
18 retirement act of 1979, 1980 PA 300, MCL 38.1301 to 38.1437.
19 (e) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
20 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
21 subcommittees on community colleges, the senate and house fiscal
22 agencies, the senate and house policy offices, and the state budget
23 office.

13 Sec. 212. Community college districts are encouraged to
14 evaluate and pursue efficiency and cost-containment measures that
15 maximize state funding. Community colleges shall identify practices
16 that increase efficiencies, including, but not limited to,
17 establishing joint ventures, consolidating services, utilizing
18 program collaborations, maximizing educational benefits through
19 optimal class sizes and frequency of course offerings, increasing
20 web-based instruction, eliminating low-enrollment and high-cost
21 instructional programs, using self-insurance, practicing energy
22 conservation, and utilizing group purchasing. Community colleges
23 shall also review proposed capital outlay projects to increase
24 coordination and utilization of new facilities, renovation
25 projects, and technology improvements.

1 paid by a full-time in-district student and a full-time out-of-
2 district student as established by the community college governing

14 (2) Each community college that receives an appropriation in
15 section 201 shall certify to the state budget director by the first
16 last business day of November August of each year that its board
17 will not adopt an increase in tuition and fee rates for in-district
18 students for the academic year that is greater than the tuition
19 restraint described in this subsection. For the academic year 2025-
20 2026, the tuition restraint level is equal to the greater of 4.5%
21 or $227.00. For the academic year 2026-2027, the tuition restraint
22 level is equal to the greater of 4.0% or $199.00. For the academic
23 year 2027-2028, the tuition restraint level is equal to the greater
24 of 4.0% or $199.00. It is intended that in the next fiscal year,
25 the tuition restraint rate will be adjusted only for the subsequent
26 academic year. As used in this subsection:
27 (a) "Fee" means any board-authorized fee that will be paid by
28 more than 1/2 of all in-district students at least once during
29 their enrollment at a community college. A community college
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
462
1 increasing a fee that applies to a specific subset of students or
2 courses shall provide sufficient information to prove that the
3 increase applied to that subset will not cause the increase in the
4 average amount of board-authorized total tuition and fees paid by
5 in-district students in the academic year to exceed the limit
6 established in this section.
7 (b) "Tuition and fee rate" means the average of full-time
8 rates paid by a majority of students in each class, based on an
9 unweighted average of the rates authorized by the community college
10 board and actually charged to students, deducting any uniformly
11 rebated or refunded amounts, for the 2 semesters with the highest
12 levels of full-time equated in-district enrollment during the
13 academic year.
14 (3) Community colleges that exceed the tuition and fee rate
15 cap described in subsection (2) are not eligible to receive
16 payments under section 201f for 1-time performance funding payments
17 for fiscal year 2025-2026. 2026-2027. The state budget director
18 shall implement uniform reporting requirements to ensure that a
19 community college receiving a payment under section 201f for 1-time
20 performance funding has satisfied the tuition restraint
21 requirements of this section. The state budget director has the
22 sole authority to determine if a community college has met the
23 requirements of this section. Information reported by a community
24 college to the state budget director under this subsection must
25 also be reported to the house and senate appropriations
26 subcommittees on community colleges and the house and senate fiscal
27 agencies.standard report recipients.
28 (4) Notwithstanding any other provision of this act, the
29 legislature may at any time adjust appropriations for a community
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
463
1 college that adopts an increase in tuition and fee rates for in-
2 district students that exceeds the rate cap established in
3 subsection (2).

21 Sec. 230. (1) Subject to subsection (4), money included in the
22 appropriations for community college operations under section 201
23 for performance funding and allocated under section 201f for 1-time
24 performance funding payments is distributed based on the following
25 formula:
26 (a) Allocated proportionate to fiscal year 2024-2025 2025-2026
27 base appropriations, 30%.
28 (b) Based on a weighted student contact hour formula as
29 provided for in the 2016 recommendations of the performance
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
471
1 indicators task force, 30%.
2 (c) Based on the performance improvement as provided for in
3 the 2016 recommendations of the performance indicators task force
4 and based on data provided by the center, 10%.
5 (d) Based on the performance completion number as provided for
6 in the 2016 recommendations of the performance indicators task
7 force, 10%.
8 (e) Based on the performance completion rate as provided for
9 in the 2016 recommendations of the performance indicators task
10 force and based on data provided by the center, 10%.
11 (f) Based on administrative costs, 5%.
12 (g) Based on the local strategic value component, as developed
13 in cooperation with the Michigan Community College Association and
14 described in subsection (2), 5%.
15 (2) Money included in the appropriations for community college
16 operations under section 201(2) and 201f for local strategic value
17 is allocated only to each community college that certifies to the
18 state budget director, through a board of trustees resolution on or
19 before October 15, 2025, 2026, that the college has met 4 out of 5
20 best practices listed in each category described in subsection (3).
21 The resolution must provide specifics as to how the community
22 college meets each best practice measure within each category. One-
23 third of funding available under the strategic value component is
24 allocated to each category described in subsection (3). Amounts
25 distributed under local strategic value must be on a proportionate
26 basis to each college's fiscal year 2024-2025 2025-2026 operations
27 funding. Payments to community colleges that qualify for local
28 strategic value funding must be distributed with the November
29 installment payment described in section 206.
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
472
1 (3) For purposes of subsection (2), the following categories
2 of best practices reflect functional activities of community
3 colleges that have strategic value to the local communities and
4 regional economies:
5 (a) For Category A, economic development and business or
6 industry partnerships, the following:
7 (i) The community college has active partnerships with local
8 employers including hospitals and health care providers.
9 (ii) The community college provides customized on-site training
10 for area companies, employees, or both.
11 (iii) The community college supports entrepreneurship through a
12 small business assistance center or other training or consulting
13 activities targeted toward small businesses.
14 (iv) The community college supports technological advancement
15 through industry partnerships, incubation activities, or operation
16 of a Michigan technical education center or other advanced
17 technology center.
18 (v) The community college has active partnerships with local
19 or regional workforce and economic development agencies.
20 (b) For Category B, educational partnerships, the following:
21 (i) The community college has active partnerships with regional
22 high schools, intermediate school districts, and career-tech
23 centers to provide instruction through dual enrollment, concurrent
24 enrollment, direct credit, middle college, or academy programs.
25 (ii) The community college hosts, sponsors, or participates in
26 enrichment programs for area K-12 students, such as college days,
27 summer or after-school programming, or Science Olympiad.
28 (iii) The community college provides, supports, or participates
29 in programming to promote successful transitions to college for
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
473
1 traditional age students, including grant programs such as talent
2 search, upward bound, or other activities to promote college
3 readiness in area high schools and community centers.
4 (iv) The community college provides, supports, or participates
5 in programming to promote successful transitions to college for new
6 or reentering adult students, such as adult basic education, a high
7 school equivalency test preparation program and testing, or
8 recruiting, advising, or orientation activities specific to adults.
9 As used in this subparagraph, "high school equivalency test
10 preparation program" means that term as defined in section 4.
11 (v) The community college has active partnerships with
12 regional 4-year colleges and universities to promote successful
13 transfer, such as articulation, 2+2, or reverse transfer agreements
14 or operation of a university center.
15 (c) For Category C, community services, the following:
16 (i) The community college provides continuing education
17 programming for leisure, wellness, personal enrichment, or
18 professional development.
19 (ii) The community college operates or sponsors opportunities
20 for community members to engage in activities that promote leisure,
21 wellness, cultural or personal enrichment such as community sports
22 teams, theater or musical ensembles, or artist guilds.
23 (iii) The community college operates public facilities to
24 promote cultural, educational, or personal enrichment for community
25 members, such as libraries, computer labs, performing arts centers,
26 museums, art galleries, or television or radio stations.
27 (iv) The community college operates public facilities to
28 promote leisure or wellness activities for community members,
29 including gymnasiums, athletic fields, tennis courts, fitness
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
474
1 centers, hiking or biking trails, or natural areas.
2 (v) The community college promotes, sponsors, or hosts
3 community service activities for students, staff, or community
4 members.
5 (4) Payments for performance funding under section 201 and for
6 1-time performance funding payments under section 201f must be made
7 to a community college only if that community college actively
8 participates in the Michigan Transfer Network sponsored by the
9 Michigan Association of Collegiate Registrars and Admissions
10 Officers and submits timely updates, including updated course
11 equivalencies at least every 6 months, to the Michigan Transfer
12 Network. The state budget director shall determine if a community
13 college has not satisfied this requirement. The state budget
14 director may withhold payments for performance funding under
15 section 201 and 1-time performance funding under section 201f until
16 a community college is in compliance with this subsection.

24 Sec. 236j. (1) The postsecondary scholarship fund is created
25 in the department of treasury for the purpose of providing
26 scholarship awards to eligible students who attend eligible
27 postsecondary educational institutions in this state, as provided
28 in subsection (5).
29 (2) The state treasurer may receive money or other assets from
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
483
1 any source for deposit into the postsecondary scholarship fund. The
2 state treasurer shall direct the investment of the postsecondary
3 scholarship fund. The state treasurer shall credit to the
4 postsecondary scholarship fund interest and earnings from
5 postsecondary scholarship fund investments.
6 (3) Money in the postsecondary scholarship fund at the close
7 of the fiscal year must remain in the postsecondary scholarship
8 fund and not lapse to the general fund.
9 (4) The department of treasury is the administrator of the
10 postsecondary scholarship fund for auditing purposes.
11 (5) Money must be expended from the postsecondary scholarship
12 fund only for the purpose of providing Michigan achievement
13 scholarship awards to eligible students who attend eligible
14 postsecondary educational institutions in this state and for other
15 purposes described in this section. From the funds appropriated in
16 section 236(7) for the Michigan achievement scholarship, the
17 department of lifelong education, advancement, and potential may
18 use up to $10,000,000.00 annually for the purposes of outreach
19 programs to raise awareness of the Michigan achievement scholarship
20 and other state scholarship programs allocated in section 236(7).
21 The department of lifelong education, advancement, and potential
22 shall ensure that state scholarships are well publicized and that
23 high school students are provided information on the availability
24 of financial aid. The department of lifelong education,
25 advancement, and potential may receive and expend funds received
26 from outside sources for scholarships, marketing, or other purposes
27 related to Michigan state scholarships. The department of lifelong
28 education, advancement, and potential shall provide the necessary
29 funding and staff to fully operate the programs.supporting state
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
484
1 grants and financial aid allocated in section 236(7).
2 (6) For the fiscal year ending September 30, 2026,
3 $300,000,000.00 of ongoing funding is deposited into the
4 postsecondary scholarship fund from the following:
5 (a) State school aid fund, $200,000,000.00.
6 (b) State general fund/general purpose money, $100,000,000.00.
7 (6) (7) It is the intent of the legislature that the
8 postsecondary scholarship fund serves as the primary a funding
9 source of the Michigan achievement scholarship. To ensure the
10 Michigan achievement scholarship provides ongoing supports for
11 students, it is the intent of the legislature to increase annual
12 deposits into the postsecondary scholarship fund until the fully
13 implemented costs of the Michigan achievement scholarship are
14 deposited annually into the postsecondary scholarship fund.provide
15 annual ongoing increases for the Michigan achievement scholarship
16 allocated in section 236(7) and to use funds from the postsecondary
17 scholarship fund to support the scale up to the fully implemented
18 costs of the Michigan achievement scholarship.

20 to prove that the increase applied to that subset will not cause

22 tuition and fees paid by resident undergraduate students in the
23 academic year to exceed the limit established in this subsection.
24 (b) "Tuition and fee rate" means the average of full-time
25 rates paid by a majority of students in each undergraduate class,
26 based on an unweighted average of the rates authorized by the
27 public university board and actually charged to students, deducting
28 any uniformly rebated or refunded amounts, for the 2 semesters with
29 the highest levels of full-time equated resident undergraduate
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

1 enrollment during the academic year, as described in the higher
2 education institutional data inventory (HEIDI) user manual.

6 (a) The public university participates in reverse transfer
7 agreements described in section 286 with at least 3 community
8 colleges in this state.
9 (b) The public university does not and will not apply any of
10 the following criteria when determining whether credits earned
11 outside the public university by a student count toward a degree or
12 certificate program offered by the public university:
13 (i) Whether the credits were earned in a dual enrollment
14 program that counted the credits toward high school graduation
15 requirements.
16 (ii) Whether the credits were earned in a course that was
17 delivered in a high school classroom, community college classroom
18 or campus, or another location.
19 (iii) Whether the credits were earned in a course that was
20 delivered online, in person, or hybrid.
21 (iv) Whether other students enrolled in the course in which the
22 credits were earned were enrolled in high school or counted the
23 course toward high school graduation requirements.
24 (c) The public university actively participates in and submits
25 timely updates to the Michigan Transfer Network created as part of
26 the Michigan Association of Collegiate Registrars and Admissions

22 (2) As used in this section:
23 (a) "Cost of attendance" means expenses for a student's
24 tuition, mandatory fees, and contact hours for the student's actual
25 program of study; books, supplies, and equipment required for
26 courses of instruction; housing and food costs; transportation
27 expenses; federal student loan fees; miscellaneous expenses,
28 including a reasonable amount for the documented cost of a personal
29 computer, allowance for child care, or allowance for other
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
507
1 dependent care; costs related to a disability; costs of obtaining a
2 license, certification, or first professional credential; and
3 reasonable costs for study abroad programs.
4 (b) "Department" means the department of lifelong education,
5 advancement, and potential.
6 (c) "Eligible institution" means a public university that
7 receives an appropriation in section 236, a community college that
8 receives an appropriation in section 201, a federally recognized
9 tribal college in this state, or an independent nonprofit college
10 or university in this state as described in section 1 of 1966 PA
11 313, MCL 390.991.
12 (d) "Gift aid" includes federal Pell grants under 20 USC
13 1070a, tuition incentive program benefits under section 256, state
14 tuition grants under section 252, awards received for minimum
15 payments awarded in subsection (4), higher education expenses paid
16 under the Michigan promise zone authority act, 2008 PA 549, MCL
17 390.1661 to 390.1679, and all other federal, state, local, or
18 institutional aid in the form of grants, scholarships, or discounts
19 applied only toward tuition and mandatory fees. Gift aid does not
20 include student loans, work-study awards, qualified withdrawals
21 made from education savings accounts to pay higher education
22 expenses pursuant to the Michigan education savings program act,
23 2000 PA 161, MCL 390.1471 to 390.1486, or higher education expenses
24 paid under the Michigan education trust program pursuant to the
25 Michigan education trust act, 1986 PA 316, MCL 390.1421 to
26 390.1442.
27 (e) "High school equivalency certificate" means that term as
28 defined in section 4.
29 (f) "Last-dollar payment amount" means 1 of the following:
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
508
1 (i) For a student attending a community college or federally
2 recognized tribal college, an amount equal to the student's
3 tuition, mandatory fees, and contact hours for the student's actual
4 program of study, minus all gift aid received by the student.
5 (ii) For a student attending a public university or an
6 independent nonprofit college or university, or for a student
7 enrolled in a baccalaureate degree program described in section 121
8 of the community college act of 1966, 1966 PA 331, MCL 389.121, an
9 amount equal to the student's individual cost of attendance, minus
10 all gift aid received by the student.
11 (g) "Minimum payment" means a payment for any eligible cost
12 within the student's individual cost of attendance. The minimum
13 payment must be awarded as a separate payment not included in the
14 student's need-based financial aid. The minimum payment must not be
15 reduced.
