26 allocation as required in the children of veterans tuition grant
27 act, 2005 PA 248, MCL 390.1341 to 390.1346.
28 Sec. 256. (1) The funds appropriated in section 236 for the
29 tuition incentive program must be distributed as provided in this
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
523
1 section and pursuant to the administrative procedures for the
2 tuition incentive program of the department of lifelong education,
3 advancement, and potential.
4 (2) As used in this section:
5 (a) "Phase I" means the first part of the tuition incentive
6 program defined as the academic period of 80 semester or 120 term
7 credits, or less, leading to an associate degree or certificate.
8 Students must be enrolled in a certificate or associate degree
9 program and taking classes within the program of study for a
10 certificate or associate degree. Tuition will not be covered for
11 courses outside of a certificate or associate degree program.
12 (b) "Phase II" means the second part of the tuition incentive
13 program that provides assistance in the third and fourth year of 4-
14 year degree programs.
15 (c) "Department" means the department of lifelong education,
16 advancement, and potential.
17 (d) "High school equivalency certificate" means that term as
18 defined in section 4.
19 (3) An individual must meet the following basic criteria and
20 financial thresholds to be eligible for tuition incentive program
21 benefits:
22 (a) To be eligible for phase I, an individual must meet all of
23 the following criteria:
24 (i) Be less than 20 years of age at the time the individual
25 graduates from high school with a diploma or certificate of
26 completion or achieves a high school equivalency certificate or,
27 for students attending a 5-year middle college approved by the
28 Michigan department of education, be less than 21 years of age when
29 the individual graduates from high school, or be considered a
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
524
1 special education pupil at the time the individual graduated from
2 high school as described in section 6(4)(l)(i).
3 (ii) Be a United States citizen and maintain residency in this
4 state, as determined for purposes of the Free Application for
5 Federal Student Aid.
6 (iii) Be at least a half-time student, earning less than 80
7 semester or 120 term credits at a participating educational
8 institution within 4 years of high school graduation or achievement
9 of a high school equivalency certificate. All program eligibility
10 expires 10 years after initial enrollment at a participating
11 educational institution.
12 (iv) Meet the satisfactory academic progress policy of the
13 educational institution attended by the individual.
14 (b) To be eligible for phase II, an individual must meet
15 either of the following criteria in addition to the criteria in
16 subdivision (a):
17 (i) Complete at least 56 transferable semester or 84
18 transferable term credits.
19 (ii) Obtain an associate degree or certificate at a
20 participating institution.
21 (c) To be eligible for phase I or phase II, an individual must
22 be financially eligible as determined by the department. An
23 individual is financially eligible for the tuition incentive
24 program if the individual was eligible for Medicaid from this state
25 for 24 months within any 36 months prior to completion of high
26 school or achievement of a high school equivalency certificate. The
27 department shall accept certification of Medicaid eligibility only
28 from the department of health and human services for the purposes
29 of verifying if a person is Medicaid eligible for 24 months within
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
525
1 any 36 months prior to completion of high school or achievement of
2 a high school equivalency certificate. Certification of eligibility
3 may begin in the sixth grade.
4 (4) For phase I, the department shall provide payment on
5 behalf of a person eligible under subsection (3). The department
6 shall only accept standard per-credit hour tuition billings and
7 shall reject billings that are excessive or outside the guidelines
8 for the type of educational institution.
9 (5) For phase I, all of the following apply:
10 (a) Payments for associate degree or certificate programs must
11 not be made for more than 80 semester or 120 term credits for any
12 individual student at any participating institution. The department
13 shall not do either of the following:
14 (i) Adopt or apply any total semester-credit or term-credit
15 maximum that is less than the 80 semester-credit or 120 term-credit
16 maximum provided in this subdivision.
17 (ii) Adopt or apply any per-semester or per-term credit maximum
18 for a student whose semester-credit or term-credit load will not
19 result in exceeding the total 80 semester-credit or 120 term-credit
20 maximum provided in this subdivision.
21 (b) For persons enrolled at a Michigan community college, the
22 department shall pay the current in-district tuition and mandatory
23 fees. For persons residing in an area that is not included in any
24 community college district, the out-of-district tuition rate may be
25 authorized.
26 (c) For persons enrolled at a Michigan public university, the
27 department shall pay lower division resident tuition and mandatory
28 fees for the current year.
29 (d) For persons enrolled at a Michigan independent, nonprofit
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
526
1 degree-granting college or university or a Michigan federal
2 tribally controlled community college, the department shall pay
3 mandatory fees for the current year and a per-credit payment that
4 does not exceed the average community college in-district per-
5 credit tuition rate as reported by the last business day of August
6 for the immediately preceding academic year.
7 (6) A person participating in phase II may be eligible for
8 additional funds not to exceed $500.00 per semester or $400.00 per
9 term up to a maximum of $2,000.00 subject to the following
10 conditions:
11 (a) Credits are earned in a 4-year program at a Michigan
12 degree-granting 4-year college or university.
13 (b) The tuition reimbursement is for coursework completed
14 within 30 months of completion of the phase I requirements.
15 (7) The department shall work closely with participating
16 institutions to provide the highest level of participation and
17 ensure that all requirements of the program are met.
18 (8) The department shall notify students in an age-appropriate
19 manner of their financial eligibility for the program any time
20 after the student begins sixth grade.
21 (9) Except as otherwise provided in sections 13(c) and 17 of
22 the Michigan reconnect grant recipient act, 2020 PA 68, MCL
23 390.1713 and 390.1717, each institution shall ensure that all known
24 available restricted grants for tuition and fees are used before
25 billing the tuition incentive program for any portion of a
26 student's tuition and fees.
27 (10) The department shall ensure that the tuition incentive
28 program is well publicized and that eligible Medicaid clients are
29 provided information on the program. The department shall provide
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
527
1 the necessary funding and staff to fully operate the program.
2 (11) The department shall collaborate with the center to use
3 the P-20 longitudinal data system to report the following
4 information for each qualified postsecondary institution:
5 (a) The number of phase I students in the most recently
6 completed academic year who in any academic year received a tuition
7 incentive program award and who successfully completed a degree or
8 certificate program. Cohort graduation rates for phase I students
9 must be calculated using the established success rate methodology
10 developed by the center in collaboration with the postsecondary
11 institutions.
12 (b) The number of students in the most recently completed
13 academic year who in any academic year received a Pell grant at the
14 reporting institution and who successfully completed a degree or
15 certificate program. Cohort graduation rates for students who
16 received Pell grants must be calculated using the established
17 success rate methodology developed by the center in collaboration
18 with the postsecondary institutions.
19 (12) If a qualified postsecondary institution does not report
20 the data necessary to complete the reporting in subsection (11) to
21 the P-20 longitudinal data system by October 15 for the prior
22 academic year, the department shall not award phase I tuition
23 incentive program funding to otherwise eligible students enrolled
24 in that institution until the data are submitted.
25 (13) By May 14, 2027, the department must compile a report,
26 and submit that report to the standard report recipients, that
27 includes data and trends regarding students who are eligible for
28 phase I or phase II awards under this section and who are also
29 eligible for the Michigan reconnect program as outlined in section
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
528
1 247, or the Michigan achievement scholarship as outlined in section
2 248, or both. Using state scholarship data from preceding fiscal
3 years, the report shall recommend changes to the tuition incentive
4 program that would improve coordination among state financial aid
5 programs, reduce ongoing costs and state financial aid scholarship
6 duplication, increase student access to postsecondary education,
7 improve on-time completion of postsecondary credentials and
8 degrees, and advance the statewide postsecondary attainment goal
9 described in sections 226e and 275j of this act. The department
10 must coordinate with stakeholders from public and private
11 institutions of higher education in this state and other relevant
12 stakeholders.

13 received under section 278, 279, or 281.
14 (2) Funds received for the purpose of administering programs
15 under sections 278, 279, and 281 must not be used for direct
16 financial aid or indirect financial aid. However, a public
17 university may provide academic incentives to motivate
18 participating students as approved by the department. As used in
19 this subsection:
20 (a) "Direct financial aid" includes, but is not limited to,
21 scholarships, payment of tuition, stipends, and work-studies.
22 (b) "Indirect financial aid" includes, but is not limited to,
23 transportation, textbook allowances, child care support, and
24 assistance with medical premiums or expenses.
25 ARTICLE IV
26 Sec. 295. The state budget director shall take steps to ensure
27 that all state fiscal recovery funds allocated to this state under
28 the American rescue plan act of 2021, Public Law 117-2,
29 are expended by December 31, 2026, as required by law. Any state
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
542
1 fiscal recovery funds that would otherwise lapse after September
2 30, 2026 are automatically reappropriated for the same purpose as
3 originally authorized and available for expenditure through
4 December 31, 2026 and any subsequent financial closeout period.
5 Sec. 295a. (1) The state budget director shall take steps to
6 ensure that all state fiscal recovery funds allocated to this state
7 under the American rescue plan act of 2021, Public Law 117-2, are
8 expended by December 31, 2026, as required by law. The state budget
9 director may reallocate appropriated funds for the purpose of fully
10 utilizing state fiscal recovery funds that are in jeopardy of not
11 meeting the expenditure deadline for reasons that may include, but
12 are not limited to, completed projects coming in under budget or
13 funds unable to be fully used by subrecipients. The state budget
14 director shall reallocate any of the funds reallocated under this
15 subsection to the programs or purposes specified in this section.
16 Any funds reallocated are unappropriated and immediately
17 reappropriated for the following purposes:
18 (a) To reclassify general fund/general purpose appropriations
19 for payroll and covered benefits for eligible public health and
20 safety employees at the department of corrections.
21 (b) To reclassify general fund/general purpose appropriations
22 for payroll and covered benefits for eligible public health and
23 safety employees at the department of state police.
24 (2) All applicable guidance, implementation, and reporting
25 provisions of Public Law 117-2 must be followed for state fiscal
26 recovery funds reallocated and reappropriated under subsection (1).
27 (3) The state budget director shall notify the senate and
28 house appropriations committees not later than 10 business days
29 after making any reallocations under subsection (1). The
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow
543
1 notification must include the authorized program under which funds
2 were originally appropriated, the amount of the reallocation, the
3 program, or programs, or purpose, and the department to which the
4 funds are being reallocated under subsection (1), and the amount
5 reallocated to each program or purpose.

between the two Houses concerning

act of 1979," by amending sections 6, 11, 11a, 11j, 11k, 11m,

First: That the Senate recede from the Substitute of the

the House as passed by the House, amended to read as follows:

bill to read as follows:
A bill to amend 1979 PA 94, entitled "AN ACT to make
appropriations to aid in the support of the public schools, the
intermediate school districts, community colleges, and public
universities of the state; to make appropriations for certain
other purposes relating to education; to provide for the
disbursement of the appropriations; to authorize the issuance of
certain bonds and provide for the security of those bonds; to

state board of education, and certain other boards and officials;

THE PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF MICHIGAN ENACT:
1 Sec. 3. (1) "Average daily attendance", for the purposes of
2 complying with federal law, means 92% of the pupils counted in
3 membership on the pupil membership count day, as defined in section
4 6(7).
5 (2) "Board" means the governing body of a district or public
6 school academy.
7 (3) "Center" means the center for educational performance and
8 information created in section 94a.
9 (4) "Community district" means a school district organized
10 under part 5b of the revised school code, MCL 380.381 to 380.396.
11 (5) "Cooperative education program" means a written voluntary
12 agreement between and among districts to provide certain
13 educational programs for pupils in certain groups of districts. The
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

4
1 written agreement must be approved by all affected districts at
2 least annually and must specify the educational programs to be
3 provided and the estimated number of pupils from each district who
4 will participate in the educational programs.
5 (6) "Department", except as otherwise provided in this
6 article, means the department of education.
7 (7) "District" means, except as otherwise specifically
8 provided in this act, a local school district established under the
9 revised school code or, except in sections 6(4), 6(6), 11x, 11y,
10 11aa, 12c, 13, 20, 22a, 22p, 31a, 51a(14), 105, 105c, and 166b, a
11 public school academy. Except in section 20, district also includes
12 a community district.
13 (8) "District of residence", except as otherwise provided in
14 this subsection, means the district in which a pupil's custodial
15 parent or parents or legal guardian resides. For a pupil described
16 in section 24b, the pupil's district of residence is the district
17 in which the pupil enrolls under that section. For a pupil
18 described in section 6(4)(d), the pupil's district of residence is
19 considered to be the district or intermediate district in which the
20 pupil is counted in membership under that section. For a pupil
21 under court jurisdiction who is placed outside the district in
22 which the pupil's custodial parent or parents or legal guardian
23 resides, the pupil's district of residence is considered to be the
24 educating district or educating intermediate district.
25 (9) "District superintendent" means the superintendent of a
26 district or the chief administrator of a public school academy.
27 Sec. 6. (1) "Center program" means a program operated by a
28 district or by an intermediate district for special education
29 pupils from several districts in programs for pupils with autism
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

5
1 spectrum disorder, pupils with severe cognitive impairment, pupils
2 with moderate cognitive impairment, pupils with severe multiple
3 impairments, pupils with hearing impairment, pupils with visual
4 impairment, and pupils with physical impairment or other health
5 impairment. Programs for pupils with emotional impairment housed in
6 buildings that do not serve regular education pupils also qualify.
7 Unless otherwise approved by the department, a center program
8 either serves all constituent districts within an intermediate
9 district or serves several districts with less than 50% of the
10 pupils residing in the operating district. In addition, special
11 education center program pupils placed part-time in noncenter
12 programs to comply with the least restrictive environment
13 provisions of section 1412 of the individuals with disabilities
14 education act, 20 USC 1412, may be considered center program pupils
15 for pupil accounting purposes for the time scheduled in either a
16 center program or a noncenter program.
17 (2) "District and high school graduation rate" means the
18 annual completion and pupil dropout rate that is calculated by the
19 center pursuant to nationally recognized standards.
20 (3) "District and high school graduation report" means a
21 report of the number of pupils, excluding adult education
22 participants, in the district for the immediately preceding school
23 year, adjusted for those pupils who have transferred into or out of
24 the district or high school, who leave high school with a diploma
25 or other credential of equal status.
26 (4) "Membership", except as otherwise provided in this
27 article, means for a district, a public school academy, or an
28 intermediate district the sum of the product of .90 times the
29 number of full-time equated pupils in grades K to 12 actually
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

6
1 enrolled and in regular daily attendance in the district, public
2 school academy, or intermediate district on the pupil membership
3 count day for the current school year, plus the product of .10
4 times the final audited count from the supplemental count day of
5 full-time equated pupils in grades K to 12 actually enrolled and in
6 regular daily attendance in the district, public school academy, or
7 intermediate district for the immediately preceding school year. A
8 district's, public school academy's, or intermediate district's
9 membership is adjusted as provided under section 25e for pupils who
10 enroll after the pupil membership count day in a strict discipline
11 academy operating under sections 1311b to 1311m of the revised
12 school code, MCL 380.1311b to 380.1311m. All pupil counts used in
13 this subsection are as determined by the department and calculated
14 by adding the number of pupils registered for attendance plus
15 pupils received by transfer and minus pupils lost as defined by
16 rules promulgated by the superintendent, and as corrected by a
17 subsequent department audit. The amount of the foundation allowance
18 for a pupil in membership is determined under section 20. In making
19 the calculation of membership, all of the following, as applicable,
20 apply to determining the membership of a district, a public school
21 academy, or an intermediate district:
22 (a) Except as otherwise provided in this subsection, and
23 pursuant to subsection (6), a pupil is counted in membership in the
24 pupil's educating district or districts. Except as otherwise
25 provided in this subsection, an individual pupil must not be
26 counted for more than a total of 1.0 full-time equated membership.
27 (b) If a pupil is educated in a district other than the
28 pupil's district of residence, if the pupil is not being educated
29 as part of a cooperative education program, if the pupil's district
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

7
1 of residence does not give the educating district its approval to
2 count the pupil in membership in the educating district, and if the
3 pupil is not covered by an exception specified in subsection (6) to
4 the requirement that the educating district must have the approval
5 of the pupil's district of residence to count the pupil in
6 membership, the pupil is not counted in membership in any district.
7 (c) A special education pupil educated by the intermediate
8 district is counted in membership in the intermediate district.
9 (d) A pupil placed by a court or state agency in an on-grounds
10 program of a juvenile detention facility, a child caring
11 institution, or a mental health institution, or a pupil funded
12 under section 53a, is counted in membership in the district or
13 intermediate district approved by the department to operate the
14 program.
15 (e) A pupil enrolled in the Michigan Schools for the Deaf and
16 Blind is counted in membership in the pupil's intermediate district
17 of residence.
18 (f) A pupil enrolled in a career and technical education
19 program supported by a millage levied over an area larger than a
20 single district or in an area vocational-technical education
21 program established under section 690 of the revised school code,
22 MCL 380.690, is counted in membership only in the pupil's district
23 of residence.
24 (g) A pupil enrolled in a public school academy is counted in
25 membership in the public school academy.
26 (h) For the purposes of this section and section 6a, for a
27 cyber school, as that term is defined in section 551 of the revised
28 school code, MCL 380.551, that is in compliance with section 553a
29 of the revised school code, MCL 380.553a, a pupil's participation
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

8
1 in the cyber school's educational program is considered regular
2 daily attendance, and for a district or public school academy, a
3 pupil's participation in a virtual course as that term is defined
4 in section 21f is considered regular daily attendance. Beginning
5 July 1, 2021, this subdivision is subject to section 8c. It is the
6 intent of the legislature that the immediately preceding sentence
7 apply retroactively and is effective July 1, 2021. For the purposes
8 of this subdivision, for a pupil enrolled in a cyber school, all of
9 the following apply with regard to the participation requirement as
10 described in this subdivision:
11 (i) Except as otherwise provided in this subdivision, the pupil
12 shall participate in each scheduled course on pupil membership
13 count day or supplemental count day, as applicable. If the pupil is
14 absent on pupil membership count day or supplemental count day, as
15 applicable, the pupil must attend and participate in class during
16 the next 10 consecutive school days if the absence was unexcused,
17 or during the next 30 calendar days if the absence was excused.
18 (ii) For a pupil who is not learning sequentially, 1 or more of
19 the following must be met on pupil membership count day or
20 supplemental count day, as applicable, for each scheduled course to
21 satisfy the participation requirement under this subdivision:
22 (A) The pupil attended a live lesson from the teacher.
23 (B) The pupil logged into a lesson or lesson activity and the
24 login can be documented.
25 (C) The pupil and teacher engaged in a subject-oriented
26 telephone conversation.
27 (D) There is documentation of an email dialogue between the
28 pupil and teacher.
29 (E) There is documentation of activity or work between the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

9
1 learning coach and pupil.
2 (F) An alternate form of attendance as determined and agreed
3 upon by the cyber school and the pupil membership auditor was met.
4 (iii) For a pupil using sequential learning, the participation
5 requirement under this subdivision is satisfied if either of the
6 following occurs:
7 (A) Except as otherwise provided in this sub-subparagraph, the
8 pupil and the teacher of record or mentor complete a 2-way
9 interaction for 1 course during the week on which pupil membership
10 count day or supplemental count day, as applicable, occurs, and the
11 3 consecutive weeks following that week. However, if a school break
12 is scheduled during any of the weeks described in this sub-
13 subparagraph that is 4 or more days in length or instruction has
14 been canceled districtwide during any of the weeks described in
15 this sub-subparagraph for 3 or more school days, the district is
16 not required to ensure that the pupil and the teacher of record or
17 mentor completed a 2-way interaction for that week. As used in this
18 sub-subparagraph:
19 (I) "2-way interaction" means the communication that occurs
20 between the teacher of record or mentor and pupil, where 1 party
21 initiates communication and a response from the other party follows
22 that communication. Responses as described in this sub-sub-
23 subparagraph must be to the communication initiated by the teacher
24 of record or mentor, and not some other action taken. This
25 interaction may occur through, but is not limited to, means such as
26 email, telephone, instant messaging, or face-to-face conversation.
27 A parent- or legal-guardian-facilitated 2-way interaction is
28 considered a 2-way interaction if the pupil is in any of grades K
29 to 5 and does not yet possess the skills necessary to participate
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

10
1 in 2-way interactions unassisted. The interactions described in
2 this sub-sub-subparagraph must relate to a virtual course on the
3 pupil's schedule and pertain to course content or progress.
4 (II) "Mentor" means a professional employee of the district
5 who monitors the pupil's progress, ensures the pupil has access to
6 needed technology, is available for assistance, and ensures access
7 to the teacher of record. A mentor may also be the teacher of
8 record if the mentor meets the definition of a teacher of record
9 under this sub-subparagraph and the district is the provider for
10 the course.
11 (III) "Teacher of record" means a teacher to whom all of the
12 following apply:
13 (1) The teacher is responsible for providing instruction,
14 determining instructional methods for each pupil, diagnosing
15 learning needs, assessing pupil learning, prescribing intervention
16 strategies and modifying lessons, reporting outcomes, and
17 evaluating the effects of instruction and support strategies. The
18 teacher of record may coordinate the distribution and assignment of
19 the responsibilities described in this sub-sub-sub-subparagraph
20 with other teachers participating in the instructional process for
21 a course.
22 (2) The teacher is certified for the grade level or is working
23 under a valid substitute permit, authorization, or approval issued
24 by the department.
25 (3) The teacher has a personnel identification code provided
26 by the center.
27 (IV) "Week" means a period that starts on Wednesday and ends
28 the following Tuesday.
29 (B) The pupil completes a combination of 1 or more of the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

11
1 following activities for each scheduled course on pupil membership
2 count day or supplemental count day, as applicable:
3 (I) Documented attendance in a virtual course where
4 synchronous, live instruction occurred with the teacher.
5 (II) Documented completion of a course assignment.
6 (III) Documented completion of a course lesson or lesson
7 activity.
8 (IV) Documented pupil access to an ongoing lesson, which does
9 not include a login.
10 (V) Documented physical attendance on pupil membership count
11 day or supplemental count day, as applicable, in each scheduled

16 (iv) For purposes of subparagraph (iii), each scheduled course
17 currently being attempted by the pupil, rather than every course on

19 each scheduled course for the pupil.
20 (i) For a new district or public school academy beginning its
21 operation after December 31, 1994, membership for the first 2 full
22 or partial fiscal years of operation is determined as follows:
23 (i) If operations begin before the pupil membership count day
24 for the fiscal year, membership is the average number of full-time
25 equated pupils in grades K to 12 actually enrolled and in regular
26 daily attendance on the pupil membership count day for the current
27 school year and on the supplemental count day for the current
28 school year, as determined by the department and calculated by
29 adding the number of pupils registered for attendance on the pupil
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

12
1 membership count day plus pupils received by transfer and minus
2 pupils lost as defined by rules promulgated by the superintendent,
3 and as corrected by a subsequent department audit, plus the final
4 audited count from the supplemental count day for the current
5 school year, and dividing that sum by 2.
6 (ii) If operations begin after the pupil membership count day
7 for the fiscal year and not later than the supplemental count day
8 for the fiscal year, membership is the final audited count of the
9 number of full-time equated pupils in grades K to 12 actually
10 enrolled and in regular daily attendance on the supplemental count
11 day for the current school year.
12 (j) If a district is the authorizing body for a public school
13 academy, then, in the first school year in which pupils are counted
14 in membership on the pupil membership count day in the public
15 school academy, the determination of the district's membership
16 excludes from the district's pupil count for the immediately
17 preceding supplemental count day any pupils who are counted in the
18 public school academy on that first pupil membership count day who
19 were also counted in the district on the immediately preceding
20 supplemental count day.
21 (k) For an extended school year program approved by the
22 superintendent, a pupil enrolled, but not scheduled to be in
23 regular daily attendance, on a pupil membership count day, is
24 counted in membership.
25 (l) To be counted in membership, a pupil must meet the minimum
26 age requirement to be eligible to attend school under section 1147
27 of the revised school code, MCL 380.1147, and must be less than 20
28 years of age on September 1 of the school year except as follows:
29 (i) A special education pupil who is enrolled and receiving
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

13
1 instruction in a special education program or service approved by
2 the department, who does not have a high school diploma, and who is
3 less than 26 years of age as of September 1 of the current school
4 year is counted in membership.
5 (ii) A pupil who is determined by the department to meet all of
6 the following may be counted in membership:
7 (A) Is enrolled in a public school academy or an alternative
8 education high school diploma program, that is primarily focused on
9 educating pupils with extreme barriers to education, such as being
10 homeless as that term is defined under 42 USC 11302.
11 (B) Had dropped out of school.
12 (C) Is less than 22 years of age as of September 1 of the
13 current school year.
14 (iii) If a child does not meet the minimum age requirement to be
15 eligible to attend school for that school year under section 1147

18 count the child in membership for that school year if the parent or

20 or legal guardian intends to enroll the child in kindergarten for
21 that school year.
22 (m) An individual who has achieved a high school diploma is

14
1 individual participating in a job training program funded under
2 former section 107a, as repealed by 2004 PA 351, or a jobs program
3 funded under former section 107b, administered by the department of
4 labor and economic opportunity, or participating in any successor
5 of either of those 2 programs, is not counted in membership.
6 (n) If a pupil counted in membership in a public school
7 academy is also educated by a district or intermediate district as
8 part of a cooperative education program, the pupil is counted in
9 membership only in the public school academy unless a written
10 agreement signed by all parties designates the party or parties in
11 which the pupil is counted in membership, and the instructional
12 time scheduled for the pupil in the district or intermediate
13 district is included in the full-time equated membership
14 determination under subdivision (q) and section 101. However, for
15 pupils receiving instruction in both a public school academy and in
16 a district or intermediate district but not as a part of a
17 cooperative education program, the following apply:
18 (i) If the public school academy provides instruction for at
19 least 1/2 of the class hours required under section 101, the public
20 school academy receives as its prorated share of the full-time
21 equated membership for each of those pupils an amount equal to 1
22 times the product of the hours of instruction the public school
23 academy provides divided by the number of hours required under
24 section 101 for full-time equivalency, and the remainder of the
25 full-time membership for each of those pupils is allocated to the
26 district or intermediate district providing the remainder of the
27 hours of instruction.
28 (ii) If the public school academy provides instruction for less
29 than 1/2 of the class hours required under section 101, the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

15
1 district or intermediate district providing the remainder of the
2 hours of instruction receives as its prorated share of the full-
3 time equated membership for each of those pupils an amount equal to
4 1 times the product of the hours of instruction the district or
5 intermediate district provides divided by the number of hours
6 required under section 101 for full-time equivalency, and the
7 remainder of the full-time membership for each of those pupils is
8 allocated to the public school academy.
9 (o) An individual less than 16 years of age as of September 1
10 of the current school year who is being educated in an alternative
11 education program is not counted in membership if there are also
12 adult education participants being educated in the same program or
13 classroom.
14 (p) The department shall give a uniform interpretation of
15 full-time and part-time memberships.
16 (q) The number of class hours used to calculate full-time
17 equated memberships must be consistent with section 101. In
18 determining full-time equated memberships for pupils who are
19 enrolled in a postsecondary institution or for pupils engaged in an
20 internship or work experience under section 1279h of the revised
21 school code, MCL 380.1279h, a pupil is not considered to be less
22 than a full-time equated pupil solely because of the effect of the
23 pupil's postsecondary enrollment or engagement in the internship or
24 work experience, including necessary travel time, on the number of
25 class hours provided by the district to the pupil.
26 (r) Full-time equated memberships for pupils in kindergarten
27 are determined by dividing the number of instructional hours
28 scheduled and provided per year per kindergarten pupil by the same
29 number used for determining full-time equated memberships for
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

16
1 pupils in grades 1 to 12. However, to the extent allowable under
2 federal law, for a district or public school academy that provides
3 evidence satisfactory to the department that it used federal title
4 I money in the 2 immediately preceding school fiscal years to fund
5 full-time kindergarten, full-time equated memberships for pupils in
6 kindergarten are determined by dividing the number of class hours
7 scheduled and provided per year per kindergarten pupil by a number
8 equal to 1/2 the number used for determining full-time equated
9 memberships for pupils in grades 1 to 12. The change in the
10 counting of full-time equated memberships for pupils in
11 kindergarten that took effect for 2012-2013 is not a mandate.
12 (s) For a district or a public school academy that has pupils
13 enrolled in a grade level that was not offered by the district or
14 public school academy in the immediately preceding school year, the
15 number of pupils enrolled in that grade level to be counted in
16 membership is the average of the number of those pupils enrolled
17 and in regular daily attendance on the pupil membership count day
18 and the supplemental count day of the current school year.
19 Membership is calculated by adding the number of pupils registered
20 for attendance in that grade level on the pupil membership count
21 day plus pupils received by transfer and minus pupils lost as
22 defined by rules promulgated by the superintendent, and as
23 corrected by subsequent department audit, plus the final audited
24 count from the supplemental count day for the current school year,
25 and dividing that sum by 2.

1 (17) "State school aid fund" means the state school aid fund

20 in this subsection. For the purposes of calculations under this

29 (A) The number of membership pupils in the district who are

34
1 determined to be economically disadvantaged, as reported to the
2 center in the form and manner prescribed by the center not later

4 the immediately preceding fiscal year.

6 the number of pupils determined to be eligible based on the product
7 of the identified student percentage multiplied by the total number
8 of pupils in the district, as reported to the center in the form
9 and manner prescribed by the center not later than the fifth
10 Wednesday after the pupil membership count day of the immediately
11 preceding fiscal year. These calculations must be made at the
12 building level. This sub-subparagraph applies only to an eligible
13 district for the fiscal year immediately following the first fiscal

15 in this sub-subparagraph, "identified student percentage" means the
16 quotient of the number of pupils in an eligible district who are
17 determined to be economically disadvantaged, as reported to the
18 center in a form and manner prescribed by the center not later than
19 the fifth Wednesday after the pupil membership count day in the
20 fiscal year preceding the first fiscal year in which the eligible
21 district is in the community eligibility program, divided by the
22 total number of pupils counted in an eligible district on the pupil
23 membership count day in the fiscal year preceding the first fiscal
24 year in which the eligible district is in the community eligibility
25 program.

27 pupil membership count day of the current fiscal year, the number
28 of membership pupils in the district who are determined to be
29 economically disadvantaged, as reported to the center in the form
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

35
1 and manner prescribed by the center not later than the fifth
2 Wednesday after the supplemental count day of the current fiscal
3 year.
4 (D) If, for a particular fiscal year, the number of membership
5 pupils in a district who are determined under sub-subparagraph (A)
6 to be economically disadvantaged or to be eligible based on the
7 identified student percentage varies by more than 20 percentage
8 points from the number of those pupils in the district as
9 calculated under sub-subparagraph (A) for the immediately preceding
10 fiscal year caused by an egregious reporting error by the district,
11 the department may choose to have the calculations under sub-
12 subparagraph (A) instead be made using the number of membership
13 pupils in the district who are determined to be economically
14 disadvantaged, as reported to the center in the form and manner
15 prescribed by the center not later than the fifth Wednesday after

17 year.
18 (ii) Each district must be assigned an opportunity index score
19 each fiscal year, the value of which is the quotient of the number
20 of economically disadvantaged pupils as determined under this
21 subdivision for the district and the total number of pupils in the
22 district in the immediately preceding fiscal year, multiplied by
23 100 and rounded up to the nearest whole number. Each district must
24 be assigned an opportunity index band as follows:

26 equal to 0 but less than 20 must be assigned to band 1 and must
27 receive an additional weighted pupil membership of at least 0.1913
28 and not more than 0.1965 for each economically disadvantaged pupil.

22 after the publication of the list described in subsection (5).
23 Before reporting these graduation and dropout rates, the department
24 shall allow a school or district to appeal the calculations. The
25 department shall consider and act upon the appeal within 30 days
26 after it is submitted and shall not report these graduation and
27 dropout rates until after all appeals have been considered and
28 decided.
29 (3) By the first business day in December and by the last
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

69
1 business day in June of each year, and within 30 days of any change
2 in employment or assignment status, a district shall furnish to the
3 center, in a manner prescribed by the center, information related
4 to educational personnel and personnel vacancies as necessary for
5 reporting required by state and federal law. For the purposes of
6 this subsection, the center shall only require districts and
7 intermediate districts to report information that is not already
8 available from the office of retirement services in the department
9 of technology, management, and budget, including, but not limited
10 to, vacancy start and end dates, reasons for the vacancy, and
11 reasons for the vacancy termination.
12 (4) If a district or intermediate district fails to meet the
13 requirements of this section, the department shall withhold 5% of
14 the total funds for which the district or intermediate district
15 qualifies under this article until the district or intermediate
16 district complies with this section. If the district or
17 intermediate district does not comply with this section by the end
18 of the fiscal year, the department shall place the amount withheld
19 in an escrow account until the district or intermediate district
20 complies with this section.
21 (5) Before publishing a list of school or district
22 accountability designations as required by the no child left behind

CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

1 accountability metrics. The department shall consider and act upon

5 (6) The department shall implement statewide standard

10 or intermediate district shall implement the statewide standard

12 or intermediate district updates its education data reporting

9 (a) For a district that had a foundation allowance for the

11 foundation allowance for the immediately preceding fiscal year, the
12 district receives a foundation allowance in an amount equal to the

14 current fiscal year.

27 immediately preceding fiscal year as reported by the May revenue

29 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1367b).

1 (11) To assist the legislature in determining the target
2 foundation allowance for the subsequent fiscal year, each revenue
3 estimating conference conducted under section 367b of the
4 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1367b, must
5 calculate a pupil membership factor, a revenue adjustment factor,
6 and an index as follows:
7 (a) The pupil membership factor is computed by dividing the
8 estimated membership in the school year ending in the current
9 fiscal year, excluding intermediate district membership, by the
10 estimated membership for the school year ending in the subsequent
11 fiscal year, excluding intermediate district membership. If a
12 consensus membership factor is not determined at the revenue
13 estimating conference, the principals of the revenue estimating
14 conference shall report their estimates to the house and senate
15 subcommittees responsible for school aid appropriations not later
16 than 7 days after the conclusion of the revenue conference.
17 (b) The revenue adjustment factor is computed by dividing the
18 sum of the estimated total state school aid fund revenue for the
19 subsequent fiscal year plus the estimated total state school aid
20 fund revenue for the current fiscal year, adjusted for any change
21 in the rate or base of a tax the proceeds of which are deposited in
22 that fund and excluding money transferred into that fund from the
23 countercyclical budget and economic stabilization fund under the
24 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101 to 18.1594, by
25 the sum of the estimated total school aid fund revenue for the
26 current fiscal year plus the estimated total state school aid fund
27 revenue for the immediately preceding fiscal year, adjusted for any
28 change in the rate or base of a tax the proceeds of which are
29 deposited in that fund. If a consensus revenue factor is not
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

77
1 determined at the revenue estimating conference, the principals of
2 the revenue estimating conference shall report their estimates to
3 the house and senate subcommittees responsible for school aid
4 appropriations not later than 7 days after the conclusion of the
5 revenue conference.
6 (c) The index is calculated by multiplying the pupil
7 membership factor by the revenue adjustment factor. If a consensus
8 index is not determined at the revenue estimating conference, the
9 principals of the revenue estimating conference shall report their
10 estimates to the house and senate subcommittees responsible for
11 state school aid appropriations not later than 7 days after the
12 conclusion of the revenue conference.

22 (14) As used in this section:
23 (a) "Certified mills" means the lesser of 18 mills or the
24 number of mills of school operating taxes levied by the district in
25 1993-94.
26 (b) "Current fiscal year" means the fiscal year for which a
27 particular calculation is made.
28 (c) "Dissolved district" means a district that loses its
29 organization, has its territory attached to 1 or more other
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

78
1 districts, and is dissolved as provided under section 12 of the
2 revised school code, MCL 380.12.
3 (d) "Immediately preceding fiscal year" means the fiscal year
4 immediately preceding the current fiscal year.
5 (e) "Local portion of the district's foundation allowance"
6 means an amount that is equal to the difference between (the sum of
7 the product of the taxable value per membership pupil of all
8 property in the district that is nonexempt property times the
9 district's certified mills and, for a district with certified mills
10 exceeding 12, the product of the taxable value per membership pupil
11 of property in the district that is commercial personal property
12 times the certified mills minus 12 mills) and (the quotient of the
13 product of the captured assessed valuation under tax increment
14 financing acts times the district's certified mills divided by the
15 district's membership excluding special education pupils).
16 (f) "Membership" means the definition of that term under
17 section 6 as in effect for the particular fiscal year for which a
18 particular calculation is made.
19 (g) "Nonexempt property" means property that is not a
20 principal residence, qualified agricultural property, qualified
21 forest property, supportive housing property, industrial personal
22 property, commercial personal property, or property occupied by a
23 public school academy.

28 code, MCL 380.1211.

CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

79
1 of the territory of a dissolved district is attached under section
2 12 of the revised school code, MCL 380.12.

4 the operation costs of the district as prescribed in sections 7 and
5 18 and purposes authorized under section 1211 of the revised school
6 code, MCL 380.1211.
7 (k) "School operating taxes" means local ad valorem property

9 380.1211, and retained for school operating purposes.

13 redevelopment financing act, 1996 PA 381, MCL 125.2651 to 125.2670.
14 (m) "Taxable value per membership pupil" means taxable value,
15 as certified by the county treasurer and reported to the

18 education pupils for the school year ending in the current state
19 fiscal year.

25 Sec. 21f. (1) A primary district shall enroll an eligible
26 pupil in virtual courses in accordance with the provisions of this
27 section. A primary district shall not offer a virtual course to an
28 eligible pupil unless the virtual course is published in the
29 primary district's catalog of board-approved courses or in the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

84
1 statewide catalog of virtual courses maintained by the Michigan
2 Virtual University pursuant to section 98. The primary district
3 shall also provide on its publicly accessible website a link to the
4 statewide catalog of virtual courses maintained by the Michigan
5 Virtual University. Unless the pupil is at least age 18 or is an
6 emancipated minor, a pupil must not be enrolled in a course that
7 meets virtually for more than 6 days in a school year without the
8 consent of the pupil's parent or legal guardian.
9 (2) Subject to subsection (3), a primary district shall enroll
10 an eligible pupil in up to 2 virtual courses as requested by the
11 pupil during an academic term, semester, or trimester.
12 (3) A pupil may be enrolled in more than 2 virtual courses in
13 a specific academic term, semester, or trimester if both of the
14 following conditions are met:
15 (a) The primary district has determined that it is in the best
16 interest of the pupil.
17 (b) The pupil agrees with the recommendation of the primary
18 district.
19 (4) If the number of applicants eligible for acceptance in a
20 virtual course does not exceed the capacity of the provider to
21 provide the virtual course, the provider shall accept for
22 enrollment all of the applicants eligible for acceptance. If the
23 number of applicants exceeds the provider's capacity to provide the
24 virtual course, the provider shall use a random draw system,
25 subject to the need to abide by state and federal
26 antidiscrimination laws and court orders. A primary district that
27 is also a provider shall determine whether or not it has the
28 capacity to accept applications for enrollment from nonresident
29 applicants in virtual courses and may use that limit as the reason
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

85
1 for refusal to enroll a nonresident applicant.
2 (5) A primary district may not establish additional
3 requirements beyond those specified in this subsection that would
4 prohibit a pupil from taking a virtual course. A pupil's primary
5 district may deny the pupil enrollment in a virtual course if any
6 of the following apply, as determined by the district:
7 (a) The pupil is enrolled in any of grades K to 5.
8 (b) The pupil has previously gained the credits that would be
9 provided from the completion of the virtual course.
10 (c) The virtual course is not capable of generating academic
11 credit.
12 (d) The virtual course is inconsistent with the remaining
13 graduation requirements or career interests of the pupil.
14 (e) The pupil has not completed the prerequisite coursework
15 for the requested virtual course or has not demonstrated
16 proficiency in the prerequisite course content.
17 (f) The pupil has failed a previous virtual course in the same
18 subject during the 2 most recent academic years.
19 (g) The virtual course is of insufficient quality or rigor. A
20 primary district that denies a pupil enrollment request for this
21 reason shall enroll the pupil in a virtual course in the same or a
22 similar subject that the primary district determines is of
23 acceptable rigor and quality.
24 (h) The cost of the virtual course exceeds the amount
25 identified in subsection (10), unless the pupil or the pupil's
26 parent or legal guardian agrees to pay the cost that exceeds this
27 amount.
28 (i) The request for a virtual course enrollment did not occur
29 within the same timelines established by the primary district for
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

86
1 enrollment and schedule changes for regular courses.
2 (j) The request for a virtual course enrollment was not made
3 in the academic term, semester, trimester, or summer preceding the
4 enrollment. This subdivision does not apply to a request made by a
5 pupil who is newly enrolled in the primary district.
6 (6) If a pupil is denied enrollment in a virtual course by the
7 pupil's primary district, the primary district shall provide
8 written notification to the pupil of the denial, the reason or
9 reasons for the denial under subsection (5), and a description of
10 the appeal process. The pupil may appeal the denial by submitting a
11 letter to the superintendent of the intermediate district in which
12 the pupil's primary district is located. The letter of appeal must
13 include the reason provided by the primary district for not
14 enrolling the pupil and the reason why the pupil is claiming that
15 the enrollment should be approved. The intermediate district
16 superintendent or designee shall respond to the appeal within 5
17 days after it is received. If the intermediate district
18 superintendent or designee determines that the denial of enrollment
19 does not meet 1 or more of the reasons specified in subsection (5),
20 the primary district shall enroll the pupil in the virtual course.
21 (7) To provide a virtual course to an eligible pupil under
22 this section, a provider must do all of the following:
23 (a) Ensure that the virtual course has been published in the
24 pupil's primary district's catalog of board-approved courses or
25 published in the statewide catalog of virtual courses maintained by
26 the Michigan Virtual University.
27 (b) Assign to each pupil a teacher of record and provide the
28 primary district with the personnel identification code assigned by
29 the center for the teacher of record. If the provider is a
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

87
1 community college, the virtual course must be taught by an
2 instructor employed by or contracted through the providing
3 community college.
4 (c) Offer the virtual course on an open entry and exit method,
5 or aligned to a semester, trimester, or accelerated academic term
6 format.
7 (d) If the virtual course is offered to eligible pupils in
8 more than 1 district, the following additional requirements must
9 also be met:
10 (i) Provide the Michigan Virtual University with a course
11 syllabus that meets the definition under subsection (14)(g) in a
12 form and manner prescribed by the Michigan Virtual University for
13 inclusion in a statewide catalog of virtual courses.
14 (ii) Not later than October 1 of each fiscal year, provide the
15 Michigan Virtual University with an aggregated count of enrollments
16 for each virtual course the provider delivered to pupils under this
17 section during the immediately preceding school year, and the
18 number of enrollments in which the pupil earned 60% or more of the
19 total course points for each virtual course.
20 (8) To provide a virtual course under this section, a
21 community college shall ensure that each virtual course it provides
22 under this section generates postsecondary credit.
23 (9) For any virtual course a pupil enrolls in under this
24 section, the pupil's primary district must assign to the pupil a
25 mentor and shall supply the provider with the mentor's contact
26 information.
27 (10) For a pupil enrolled in 1 or more virtual courses, the
28 primary district shall use foundation allowance or per-pupil funds
29 calculated under section 20 to pay for the expenses associated with
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

88
1 the virtual course or courses. A primary district is not required
2 to pay toward the cost of a virtual course an amount that exceeds
3 6.67% of the target foundation allowance for the current fiscal
4 year as calculated under section 20.
5 (11) A virtual learning pupil has the same rights and access
6 to technology in the pupil's primary district's school facilities
7 as all other pupils enrolled in the pupil's primary district. The
8 department shall establish standards for hardware, software, and
9 internet access for pupils who are enrolled in more than 2 virtual
10 courses under this section in an academic term, semester, or
11 trimester taken at a location other than a school facility.
12 (12) If a pupil successfully completes a virtual course, as
13 determined by the pupil's primary district, the pupil's primary
14 district shall grant appropriate academic credit for completion of
15 the course and shall count that credit toward completion of
16 graduation and subject area requirements. A pupil's school record
17 and transcript must identify the virtual course title as it appears
18 in the virtual course syllabus.
19 (13) The enrollment of a pupil in 1 or more virtual courses
20 must not result in a pupil being counted as more than 1.0 full-time
21 equivalent pupils under this article. The minimum requirements to
22 count the pupil in membership are those established by the pupil
23 accounting manual as it was in effect for the 2015-2016 school year
24 or as subsequently amended by the department if the department
25 notifies the legislature about the proposed amendment at least 60
26 days before the amendment becomes effective.
27 (14) Subject to the requirements of this subsection, a
28 district may provide virtual instruction under this subsection for
29 not more than 6 days in a school year. If a district plans to
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

89
1 provide instruction under this subsection, the district's plan must
2 be approved by the board of the district, and the district must
3 provide notice of the plan to impacted pupils and their parents or
4 legal guardians before enactment of the plan. Instruction may be
5 provided synchronously or asynchronously, as determined by the
6 department. Days of instruction under this subsection may be used
7 only for the following purposes, as defined by the department:
8 (a) A maximum of 3 days may be designated as testing days,
9 during which students are released from regular classroom
10 instruction to participate in state or federally mandated
11 assessments. Days of virtual instruction provided under this
12 subdivision are limited to buildings and grade levels impacted by
13 assessments.
14 (b) A maximum of 3 days of virtual instruction may be provided
15 due to conditions not within control of the district, including,
16 but not limited to, closures due to snow, ice, or other extreme
17 weather events or unforeseen emergencies. Days of virtual
18 instruction under this subdivision may be used only after a
19 district has exhausted all emergency closure days permitted under
20 section 101.
21 (15) (14) As used in this section:
22 (a) "Instructor" means an individual who is employed by or
23 contracted through a community college.
24 (b) "Mentor" means a professional employee of the primary
25 district who monitors the pupil's progress, ensures the pupil has
26 access to needed technology, is available for assistance, and
27 ensures access to the teacher of record. A mentor may also serve as
28 the teacher of record if the primary district is the provider for
29 the virtual course and the mentor meets the requirements under
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

90
1 subdivision (e).
2 (c) "Primary district" means the district that enrolls the
3 pupil and reports the pupil for pupil membership purposes.
4 (d) "Provider" means the district, intermediate district,
5 community college, or other third-party vendor that the primary
6 district pays to provide the virtual course or the Michigan Virtual
7 University if it is providing the virtual course.
8 (e) "Teacher of record" means a teacher who meets all of the
9 following:
10 (i) Is appropriately placed under a valid Michigan teaching
11 certificate or a teaching permit, authorization, or approval issued
12 by the department. As used in this subparagraph, "appropriately
13 placed" means holding a valid Michigan educator credential with the
14 required grade range and discipline or subject area for the
15 assignment, as defined by the superintendent of public instruction.
16 (ii) Is responsible for providing instruction, determining
17 instructional methods for each pupil, diagnosing learning needs,
18 assessing pupil learning, prescribing intervention strategies and
19 modifying lessons, reporting outcomes, and evaluating the effects
20 of instruction and support strategies.
21 (iii) Has a personnel identification code provided by the
22 center.
23 (iv) If the provider is a community college, is an instructor
24 employed by or contracted through the providing community college.
25 (f) "Virtual course" means a course of study that is capable
26 of generating a credit or a grade and that is provided in an
27 interactive learning environment where any portion of the
28 curriculum is delivered using the internet and in which pupils may
29 be separated from their instructor or teacher of record by time or
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

91
1 location, or both.
2 (g) "Virtual course syllabus" means a document that includes
3 all of the following:
4 (i) An alignment document detailing how the course meets
5 applicable state standards or, if the state does not have state
6 standards, nationally recognized standards.
7 (ii) The virtual course content outline.
8 (iii) The virtual course required assessments.
9 (iv) The virtual course prerequisites.
10 (v) Expectations for actual instructor or teacher of record
11 contact time with the virtual learning pupil and other
12 communications between a pupil and the instructor or teacher of
13 record.
14 (vi) Academic support available to the virtual learning pupil.
15 (vii) The virtual course learning outcomes and objectives.
16 (viii) The name of the institution or organization providing the
17 virtual content.
18 (ix) The name of the institution or organization providing the
19 instructor or teacher of record.
20 (x) The course titles assigned by the provider and the course
21 titles and course codes from the National Center for Education
22 Statistics (NCES) school codes for the exchange of data (SCED).
23 (xi) The number of eligible pupils that will be accepted by the
24 provider in the virtual course. A primary district that is also the
25 provider may limit the enrollment to those pupils enrolled in the
26 primary district.
27 (xii) The results of the virtual course quality review using
28 the guidelines and model review process published by the Michigan
29 Virtual University.

1 (h) "Virtual learning pupil" means a pupil enrolled in 1 or

3 Sec. 21h. (1) From the state school aid pupil support reserve
4 fund money appropriated in section 11, there is allocated
5 $6,137,400.00 for 2025-2026 2026-2027 for assisting districts
6 assigned by the superintendent to participate in a partnership
7 district agreement to improve student achievement and district
8 financial stability. It is the intent of the legislature that the
9 appropriation in this section will be funded with state school aid
10 pupil support reserve fund money through 2027-2028. The
11 superintendent shall identify any conditions that may be
12 contributing to low academic performance within a district being
13 considered for assignment to a partnership district agreement. The
14 purpose of the partnership district agreement is to identify
15 district needs, develop intervention plans, and partner with
16 public, private, and nonprofit organizations to coordinate
17 resources and improve student achievement. Assignment of a district
18 to a partnership district agreement is made by the superintendent.
19 (2) A district described in subsection (1) is eligible for
20 funding under this section if the district includes at least 1
21 school that has been identified as low performing under the
22 approved federal accountability system. A district described in
23 this subsection must do all of the following to be eligible for
24 funding under this section:
25 (a) Within 90 days of assignment to the partnership district
26 agreement described in this section, complete a comprehensive needs
27 assessment or evaluation in collaboration with an intermediate
28 district, community members, education organizations, and
29 postsecondary institutions, as applicable, that is approved by the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

93
1 superintendent. The comprehensive needs assessment or evaluation
2 must include at least all of the following:
3 (i) A review of the district's implementation and utilization
4 of a multi-tiered system of supports to ensure that it is used to
5 appropriately inform instruction.
6 (ii) A review of the district and school building leadership
7 and educator capacity to substantially improve student outcomes.
8 (iii) A review of classroom, instructional, and operational
9 practices and curriculum to ensure alignment with research-based
10 instructional practices and state curriculum standards.
11 (b) Develop a district continuous improvement plan that has
12 been approved by the superintendent and that addresses the needs
13 identified in the comprehensive needs assessment or evaluation
14 completed under subdivision (a). The district continuous
15 improvement plan must include at least all of the following:
16 (i) Specific actions that will be taken by the district and
17 each of its partners to improve student achievement.
18 (ii) Specific measurable benchmarks that will be met within 18
19 months to improve student achievement and identification of
20 expected student achievement outcomes to be attained within 3 years
21 after assignment to the partnership.
22 (iii) Measurable benchmarks that put pupils on track to meet or
23 exceed grade level proficiency, increase high school graduation
24 rates, reduce class sizes in grades K to 3, and improve attendance
25 rates.
26 (c) Provide access to training for district leadership,
27 including, but not limited to, the superintendent or chief
28 administrator and school board or board of directors members, on
29 areas of education fiscal and policy matters. The department may
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

94
1 require training for district leadership and all board members
2 under this subdivision at a rate and frequency needed to support
3 measurable academic outcomes for the district.
4 (3) Upon approval of the district continuous improvement plan
5 developed under subsection (2), the department shall assign a team
6 of individuals with expertise in comprehensive school and district
7 reform to partner with the district, the intermediate district,
8 community organizations, education organizations, and postsecondary
9 institutions identified in the academic and financial operating or
10 intervention district continuous improvement plan to review the
11 district's use of existing financial resources to ensure that those
12 resources are being used as efficiently and effectively as possible
13 to improve student academic achievement and to ensure district
14 financial stability. The superintendent of public instruction may
15 waive burdensome administrative rules for a partnership district
16 for the duration of the partnership district agreement.
17 (4) Funds allocated under this section, excluding funds

1 data security, and student privacy issues. The integration of data

6 (7) Participation in a data hub region in the Michigan data
7 hub network under this section is voluntary and is not required.
8 (8) Entities receiving funding under this section shall use
9 the funds for all of the following:
10 (a) Creating an infrastructure that effectively manages the
11 movement of data between data systems used by intermediate
12 districts, districts, and other educational organizations in
13 Michigan based on common data standards to improve student
14 achievement.
15 (b) Utilizing the infrastructure to put in place commonly
16 needed integrations, reducing cost and effort to do that work while
17 increasing data accuracy and usability.
18 (c) Promoting the use of a more common set of applications by
19 promoting systems that integrate with the Michigan data hub
20 network.
21 (d) Promoting 100% district adoption of the Michigan data hub
22 network.
23 (e) Ensuring local control of data, data security, and student
24 data privacy.
25 (f) Utilizing the infrastructure to promote the actionable use
26 of data through common reports and dashboards that are consistent
27 statewide.
28 (g) Creating a governance model to facilitate sustainable
29 operations of the infrastructure in the future, including
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

1 administration, legal agreements, documentation, staffing, hosting,

1 educators. The digital credentialing initiative must provide

3 portable, and accessible digital record of skills, competencies,
4 and achievements that may be securely shared with employers,
5 postsecondary institutions, and workforce partners across this
6 state, supporting Michigan's economic workforce competitiveness by

16 pathway achievements, workforce readiness, and postsecondary

18 programs and statewide competency frameworks to ensure consistency

29 and displays verified credentials, skills, competencies, and

151
1 achievements and can be securely shared.

3 professional development for educators, including the completion of
4 LETRS training or other science of reading-based training.
5 Credentials issued for the completion of LETRS training must be
6 competency based, verifiable, and electronically shareable by the
7 educator. Each credential must document the specific level or unit
8 completed and the date of completion.

11 regional workforce and education systems to support Michigan's

18 outcomes.

4 count day of the current fiscal year, the number of membership
5 pupils in the district or public school academy who are determined
6 to be economically disadvantaged, as reported to the center in the
7 form and manner prescribed by the center not later than the fifth
8 Wednesday after the supplemental count day of the current fiscal
9 year.
10 (d) If, for a particular fiscal year, the number of membership
11 pupils in a district or public school academy who are determined
12 under subdivision (a) to be economically disadvantaged or to be
13 eligible based on the identified student percentage varies by more
14 than 20 percentage points from the number of those pupils in the
15 district or public school academy as calculated under subdivision
16 (a) for the immediately preceding fiscal year caused by an
17 egregious reporting error by the district or public school academy,
18 the department may choose to have the calculations under
19 subdivision (a) instead be made using the number of membership
20 pupils in the district or public school academy who are determined
21 to be economically disadvantaged, as reported to the center in the
22 form and manner prescribed by the center not later than the fifth
23 Wednesday after the supplemental count day of the immediately
24 preceding fiscal year.
25 (4) Each district or public school academy must be assigned an
26 opportunity index score each fiscal year, the value of which is the
27 quotient of the number of economically disadvantaged pupils as
28 determined under subsection (3) for the district or public school
29 academy and the total number of pupils in the district or public
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

161
1 school academy in the immediately preceding fiscal year, multiplied
2 by 100 and rounded up to the nearest whole number. Each district or
3 public school academy must be assigned an opportunity index band as
4 follows:
5 (a) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
6 index score greater than or equal to 0 but less than 20 must be
7 assigned to band 1 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
8 (3) at a rate of at least 35.0% and less than 36.0%. The
9 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
10 to the district's opportunity index score minus 1, multiplied by
11 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
12 35.0%.
13 (b) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
14 index score greater than or equal to 20 but less than 44 must be
15 assigned to band 2 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
16 (3) at a rate of at least 36.0% and less than 37.5%. The
17 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
18 to the district's opportunity index score minus 20, multiplied by
19 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
20 36.0%.
21 (c) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
22 index score greater than or equal to 44 but less than 59 must be
23 assigned to band 3 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
24 (3) at a rate of at least 37.5% and less than 39.0%. The
25 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
26 to the district's opportunity index score minus 44, multiplied by
27 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
28 37.5%.
29 (d) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

162
1 index score greater than or equal to 59 but less than 73 must be
2 assigned to band 4 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
3 (3) at a rate of at least 39.0% and less than 42.0%. The
4 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
5 to the district's opportunity index score minus 59, multiplied by
6 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
7 39.0%.
8 (e) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
9 index score greater than or equal to 73 but less than 85 must be
10 assigned to band 5 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
11 (3) at a rate of at least 42.0% and less than 47.0%. The
12 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
13 to the district's opportunity index score minus 73, multiplied by
14 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
15 42.0%.
16 (f) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
17 index score greater than or equal to 85 must be assigned to band 6
18 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection (3) at a rate of
19 47.0%.
20 (g) As used in this subsection, "band adjustment factor" means
21 an amount equal to the difference between the lowest and highest
22 reimbursement bounds for each band, divided by the number of
23 possible opportunity index scores in that band.

6 (24) As used in this section:
7 (a) "At-risk pupil" means a pupil in grades pre-K to 12 for
8 whom the district has documentation that the pupil meets any of the
9 following criteria:
10 (i) The pupil is economically disadvantaged.
11 (ii) The pupil is an English language learner.
12 (iii) The pupil is chronically absent as defined by and reported
13 to the center.
14 (iv) The pupil is a victim of child abuse or neglect.
15 (v) The pupil is a pregnant teenager or teenage parent.
16 (vi) The pupil has a family history of school failure,
17 incarceration, or substance abuse.
18 (vii) The pupil is an immigrant who has immigrated within the
19 immediately preceding 3 years.
20 (viii) The pupil did not complete high school in 4 years and is
21 still continuing in school as identified in the Michigan cohort
22 graduation and dropout report.
23 (ix) For pupils for whom the results of the state summative
24 assessment have been received, is a pupil who did not achieve
25 proficiency on the English language arts, mathematics, science, or
26 social studies content area assessment.
27 (x) Is a pupil who is at risk of not meeting the district's or
28 public school academy's core academic curricular objectives in
29 English language arts or mathematics, as demonstrated on local
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

171
1 assessments.
2 (b) "Economically disadvantaged" means a pupil who has been
3 determined eligible for free or reduced-price meals as determined
4 under the Richard B. Russell national school lunch act, 42 USC 1751
5 to 1769j; who is in a household receiving supplemental nutrition
6 assistance program or temporary assistance for needy families
7 assistance; or who is homeless, migrant, or in foster care, as
8 reported to the center.
9 (c) "English language learner" means limited English
10 proficient pupils who speak a language other than English as their
11 primary language and have difficulty speaking, reading, writing, or
12 understanding English as reported to the center.

1 activities and goals of the program described in this section. The

4 (6) For Michigan-grown fruits, vegetables, and legumes that
5 satisfy the requirements of subsection (5), the department shall
6 make matching reimbursements in an amount not to exceed 10 cents
7 for every school meal that is served as part of the United States
8 Department of Agriculture's child nutrition programs.
9 (7) In awarding grants under this section, the department
10 shall work in consultation with Michigan-based farm-to-school
11 resource organizations to develop scoring criteria that assess an
12 applicant's ability to procure Michigan-grown products, prepare and
13 menu Michigan-grown products, promote and market Michigan-grown
14 products, and submit letters of intent from districts on plans for
15 educational activities that promote the goals of the program.
16 (8) The department shall give preference to districts that
17 propose educational activities that meet 1 or more of the
18 following: promote healthy food activities; have clear educational
19 objectives; involve parents or the community; connect to a school's
20 or child care center's farm-to-school or farm-to-early-child-care
21 procurement activities; and market and promote the program, leading
22 to increased pupil knowledge and consumption of Michigan-grown
23 products. The department shall give stronger weighting and
24 consideration to applications with robust marketing and promotional
25 activities.
26 (9) In awarding grants, the department shall also consider all
27 of the following:
28 (a) The percentage of children who qualify for free or
29 reduced-price school meals under the Richard B. Russell national
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

178
1 school lunch act, 42 USC 1751 to 1769j.
2 (b) The variety of school sizes and geographic locations
3 within the identified prosperity regions.
4 (c) Existing or planned collaboration between districts, or
5 with agricultural businesses and essential local food
6 infrastructure, such as farms, farm cooperatives, processors,
7 distributors, and local food hubs.
8 (10) As a condition of receiving a grant under this section, a
9 district shall provide or direct its vendors to provide to the
10 department copies of monthly receipts that show the quantity of
11 different Michigan-grown fruits, vegetables, and legumes purchased,
12 the amount of money spent on each of these products, the name and
13 Michigan location of the farm that grew the products, and the
14 methods or plans to market and promote the program. The district
15 also shall provide to the department monthly United States
16 Department of Agriculture child nutrition reimbursable meal numbers
17 and must retain monthly menus noting when and how Michigan-grown
18 products were used in meals. The district and school food service
19 director or directors also shall agree to respond to brief online
20 surveys and to provide a report that shows the percentage
21 relationship of Michigan spending compared to total food spending.
