15 subsection:
16 (a) The comprehensive investigation will assess the
17 circumstances surrounding the mass casualty event, including, but
18 not limited to:
19 (i) Emergency response effectiveness.
20 (ii) Compliance with safety protocols.
21 (iii) Communication procedures.
22 (iv) Any factors contributing to the incident.
23 (b) The governor shall designate an appropriate person or
24 investigative entity to conduct the comprehensive investigation.
25 This person or investigative entity may include, but is not limited
26 to, state law enforcement agencies, independent review boards, or
27 specially appointed task forces. The person or designated
28 investigative entity has the authority to do all of the following:
29 (i) Access relevant records and data from the district.
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

198
1 (ii) Interview witnesses and district personnel involved.
2 (iii) Issue findings and recommendations based on the
3 investigation.
4 (c) The person or investigative entity designated in
5 subdivision (b) shall prepare a detailed report of its findings and
6 submit the report to the governor and relevant legislative
7 committees within 90 days following the conclusion of the
8 investigation. The report must include recommendations for
9 preventing future incidents and improving school safety protocols.
10 (10) Funds allocated under subsection (4) for 2025-2026 are a
11 work project appropriation, and any unexpended funds for 2025-2026
12 are carried forward into 2026-2027. The purpose of the work project
13 is to continue providing funding to support districts, intermediate
14 districts, and nonpublic schools in having school resource
15 officers. The estimated completion date of the work project is
16 September 30, 2029.
17 (11) Funds allocated under subsection (5) for 2025-2026 are a
18 work project appropriation, and any unexpended funds for 2025-2026
19 are carried forward into 2026-2027. The purpose of the work project
20 is to continue providing funding to support districts, intermediate
21 districts, and nonpublic schools in hiring staff to support student
22 mental health. The estimated completion date of the work project is
23 September 30, 2029.
24 (7) In addition to the appropriations in section 11, if the
25 general fund money allocated in subsection (1) is insufficient to
26 fully fund awards to nonpublic schools under subsections (2) and
27 (3), there is appropriated from the general pupil support reserve
28 fund created in section 22s the amount necessary, not to exceed
29 $11,000,000.00, to fund these reimbursements.
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

199
1 (8) (12) As used in this section:
2 (a) "Mass casualty event" means any of the following that
3 occur on school grounds or at a school-sponsored event:
4 (i) An incident resulting in significant physical injuries to
5 not fewer than 3 individuals. As used in this subparagraph,
6 "significant physical injury" means an injury that involves a risk
7 of death, significant physical pain, protracted and obvious
8 disfigurement, or a protracted loss or impairment of the function
9 of a bodily member, organ, or mental or sensory faculty.
10 (ii) An incident resulting in 2 or more fatalities.
11 (iii) An incident of such scope that exceeds the normal
12 resources for the emergency response available resources in the
13 jurisdiction where the incident takes place are not sufficient and
14 resources from surrounding jurisdictions are required.
15 (iv) An incident that results in a sudden and timely surge of
16 injured individuals necessitating emergency services.
17 (b) "Safety dog" means a dog that is contracted by a law
18 enforcement agency of this state, a local unit of government of
19 this state, or a district or an intermediate district and that is
20 trained for detection of firearms, explosives, narcotics, or vape
21 substances.
22 (c) "School grounds" means all properties owned or operated by
23 the district, including transportation vehicles owned or operated
24 by the district.
25 (d) "School-sponsored event" means any activity organized or
26 sanctioned by the district.
27 (9) (13) Notwithstanding section 17b, the department shall
28 make payments under this section on a schedule determined by the
29 department.

14 provider to receive a great start to quality level ahead of any
15 other type of provider.
16 (c) (d) By not later than March 1 of each year, compile the
17 results of the information reported by each intermediate district
18 or consortium under subsection (19) (16) and report to the

20 intermediate district or consortium with the number and percentage
21 of each intermediate district's or consortium's total allocation
22 allocated to community-based providers by provider type, including
23 private for-profit, private nonprofit, community college or
24 university, Head Start grantee or delegate, and district or
25 intermediate district.

1 following order of priority:

16 (20) (23) As used in this section:
17 (a) "Child care center" means that term as defined in section
18 1 of 1973 PA 116, MCL 722.111.
19 (b) "Eligible great start readiness program options" means a
20 program option that operates on a school-day, part-day, or extended
21 schedule length, as determined by the department of lifelong
22 education, advancement, and potential. The department of lifelong
23 education, advancement, and potential must maintain and publish on
24 its website requirements for each eligible schedule length,
25 including the minimum day length, the minimum number of days per
26 week, and the minimum number of weeks per year. These programs may
27 be blended with Head Start programs, if allowable by federal rules
28 and regulations.
29 (c) "Federal poverty guidelines" means the guidelines
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

218
1 published annually in the Federal Register by the United States
2 Department of Health and Human Services under its authority to
3 revise the poverty line under 42 USC 9902.
4 (d) "GSRP extended program" means a program that operates for
5 at least the same length of day as a district's first grade program
6 for a minimum of 5 days per week, 36 weeks per year.
7 (e) "GSRP/Head Start extended blended program" means a program
8 funded under this section and a Head Start program that are
9 combined for an extended program.
10 (f) "GSRP/Head Start school-day blended program" means a part-
11 day program funded under this section and a Head Start program,
12 which are combined for a school-day program.
13 (g) "Licensed child care center" means a child care center
14 that has been issued a license under 1973 PA 116, MCL 722.111 to
15 722.128, to operate a child care center.
16 (h) "Part-day program" means a program that operates at least
17 4 days per week, 30 weeks per year, for at least 3 hours of
18 teacher-child contact time per day but for fewer hours of teacher-
19 child contact time per day than a school-day program.
20 (i) "School-day program" means a program that operates for at
21 least the same length of day as a district's first grade program
22 for a minimum of 4 days per week, 30 weeks per year. A classroom
23 that offers a school-day program must enroll all children for the
24 school day to be considered a school-day program.

29 (6) (13) As used in this section:
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

260
1 (a) "Dyslexia" means both of the following:
2 (i) A specific learning disorder that is neurobiological in
3 origin and characterized by difficulties with accurate or fluent
4 word recognition and by poor spelling and decoding abilities that
5 typically result from a deficit in the phonological component of
6 language that is often unexpected in relation to other cognitive
7 abilities and the provision of effective classroom instruction.
8 (ii) A specific learning disorder that may include secondary
9 consequences, such as problems in reading comprehension and a
10 reduced reading experience that can impede the growth of vocabulary
11 and background knowledge and lead to social, emotional, and
12 behavioral difficulties.
13 (b) "Evidence-based" means an activity, program, process,
14 service, strategy, or intervention that demonstrates statistically
15 significant effects on improving pupil outcomes or other relevant
16 outcomes and that meets at least both of the following:
17 (i) At least 1 of the following:
18 (A) Is based on strong evidence from at least 1 well-designed
19 and well-implemented experimental study.
20 (B) Is based on moderate evidence from at least 1 well-
21 designed and well-implemented quasi-experimental study.
22 (C) Is based on promising evidence from at least 1 well-
23 designed and well-implemented correlational study with statistical
24 controls for selection bias.
25 (D) Demonstrates a rationale based on high-quality research
26 findings or positive evaluation that the activity, program,
27 process, service, strategy, or intervention is likely to improve
28 pupil outcomes or other relevant outcomes.
29 (ii) Includes ongoing efforts to examine the effects of the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

261
1 activity, program, process, service, strategy, or intervention.
2 (c) "Explicit" means direct and deliberate instruction through
3 continuous pupil-teacher interaction that includes teacher
4 modeling, guided practice, and independent practice.
5 (d) "Fluency" means the ability to read with speed, accuracy,
6 and proper expression.
7 (e) "Multi-tiered system of supports" means a comprehensive
8 framework that includes 3 distinct tiers of instructional support
9 and is composed of a collection of evidence-based strategies
10 designed to meet the individual needs and assets of a whole pupil
11 at all achievement levels.
12 (f) "Phonemic awareness" means the conscious awareness of all
13 of the following:
14 (i) Individual speech sounds, including, but not limited to,
15 consonants and vowels, in spoken syllables.
16 (ii) The ability to consciously manipulate through, including,
17 but not limited to, matching, blending, segmenting, deleting, or
18 substituting, individual speech sounds described in subparagraph
19 (i).
20 (iii) All levels of the speech sound system, including, but not
21 limited to, word boundaries, rhyme recognition, stress patterns,
22 syllables, onset-rime units, and phonemes.
23 (g) "Phonological" means relating to the system of contrastive
24 relationships among the speech sounds that constitute the
25 fundamental components of a language.
26 (h) "Progress monitoring" means the assessing of students'
27 academic performance, quantifying students' rates of improvement or
28 progress toward goals, and determining how students are responding
29 to instruction.
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

262
1 (i) "Rapid automatized naming (RAN)" means a task that
2 measures how quickly individuals can name objects; pictures;
3 colors; or symbols, including letters and digits, aloud, which can
4 predict later reading abilities for preliterate children.
5 Sec. 35d. (1) From the state school aid fund money
6 appropriated under section 11, there is allocated for 2026-2027
7 only an amount not to exceed $1,000,000.00 for the department to
8 provide grants to districts and intermediate districts for the
9 purchase of 1 or more components or trainings through an eligible
10 teacher training program for children with dyslexia from a provider
11 of an eligible teacher training program for children with dyslexia
12 as described in this section. It is intended that supports funded
13 under this section assist with the implementation of dyslexia
14 requirements under section 1280f of the revised school code, MCL
15 380.1280f.
16 (2) A provider that provides programming that meets all of the
17 following criteria is considered to be a provider of an eligible
18 teacher training program for purposes of this section:
19 (a) The programming allows teachers to incorporate the 5
20 components essential to an effective reading program into their
21 daily lessons. The 5 components described in this subdivision are
22 phonemic awareness, phonics, vocabulary, fluency, and
23 comprehension.
24 (b) The programming trains educators to teach reading using a
25 proven, multisensory approach.
26 (c) The programming educates teachers on how to explicitly and
27 effectively teach reading to beginning readers.
28 (d) The programming breaks reading and spelling down into
29 smaller skills involving letters and sounds, and then builds on
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

263
1 these skills over time.
2 (e) The programming uses multisensory teaching strategies to
3 teach reading by using sight, hearing, touch, and movement to help
4 students connect and learn the concepts being taught.
5 (3) Districts and intermediate districts may apply to the
6 department for grants to purchase components or training through an
7 eligible teacher training program from a provider of an eligible
8 teacher training program, and, upon receiving an application and
9 except as otherwise provided in this subsection, the department
10 shall make payments to districts and intermediate districts for
11 those purchases. The department shall make payments under this
12 section on a first-come, first-served basis until funds are
13 depleted.

19 (2) The department shall continue the committee for literacy
20 achievement that includes representatives of higher education and
21 early childhood literacy educators who have expertise in literacy
22 instruction and research. The committee shall exist until at least
23 September 2029. The department shall appoint the members of the
24 committee for literacy achievement. The department shall develop a
25 process to identify and assemble experts for the committee. The
26 primary focus of the committee is to recommend tools and strategies
27 with the goal of increasing student academic outcomes in the area
28 of literacy. The committee is encouraged to collaborate with the
29 Michigan Education Research Institute or other entities with
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

264
1 similar expertise on the subject of literacy. The department shall
2 determine the size and benchmarks of the committee. The committee
3 shall do all of the following:
4 (a) Critically evaluate early literacy series and materials.
5 The committee shall evaluate with research-based outcomes, using
6 the committee's expertise in the field of literacy. The committee
7 shall assign grade tiers to the series based on the series'
8 likelihood to increase student literacy outcomes. Priority must be
9 given to series that already have a demonstrated history of
10 increasing student outcomes. The committee may perform an
11 evaluation under this subdivision based on submissions from a
12 vendor, but must independently verify the validity of information
13 provided by the vendor. The committee is also strongly encouraged
14 to evaluate available materials not submitted to the department by
15 a vendor.
16 (b) Critically evaluate literacy professional development. The
17 committee shall evaluate with research-based outcomes, using the
18 committee's expertise in the field of literacy. The committee shall
19 assign grade tiers to the professional development based on the
20 professional development's likelihood to increase student literacy
21 outcomes. Priority must be given to professional development that
22 already has a demonstrated history of increasing student outcomes.
23 The committee may perform an evaluation under this subdivision
24 based on submissions from a vendor, but must independently verify
25 the validity of information provided by the vendor. The committee
26 is also strongly encouraged to evaluate available materials not
27 submitted to the department by a vendor.
28 (c) Critically evaluate other applicable literacy tools or
29 services the committee determines to have a high likelihood or
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

265
1 demonstrated history of increasing student literacy outcomes. The
2 committee may perform an evaluation under this subdivision based on
3 submissions from a vendor, but must independently verify the
4 validity of information provided by the vendor. The committee is
5 also strongly encouraged to evaluate available materials not
6 submitted to the department by a vendor.
7 (d) Create, maintain, and post annually on a publicly
8 available website a rankings list of all early literacy series and
9 other items the committee has evaluated. This includes items from
10 each part of the rankings list, items evaluated in the 2024-2025
11 school year, and any subsequent items evaluated going forward. The
12 committee shall denote on the list whether major changes have been
13 made to an early a literacy series or other item on the list since
14 the early literacy series or other item was evaluated by the
15 committee and if the early literacy series or other item has not
16 been evaluated since the last ranking. The rankings list created in
17 this subdivision must align with the list of evidence-based tier 1,
18 classwide elementary reading curricula and materials that are
19 aligned with science of reading methods that research has shown to
20 improve literacy outcomes and help pupils achieve reading
21 proficiency as required under section 1280f of the revised school
22 code, MCL 380.1280f. A list described in this subdivision may
23 include a maximum of 15 recommendations. Additional items may be
24 evaluated. However, only the top 15 may be recommended for use.
25 (3) An individual on the committee shall recuse themselves
26 from evaluating early literacy series and other items that the
27 individual has helped create, that the individual would benefit
28 from financially, or for which the individual has any other
29 conflict of interest.

5 (2) Eligible tutoring partners shall apply for funding in a
6 form and manner determined by the department. An application for
7 funding described in this subsection must include at least all of
8 the following:
9 (a) An identification of a district or intermediate district
10 that will serve as a fiscal agent for awarded funds.
11 (b) A list of district partners that will be recipients of
12 high-impact tutoring from the eligible tutoring partner, including
13 a letter of support from each district that affirms their
14 commitment to ensure alignment with tier 1 instruction,
15 implementation fidelity, including, but not limited to, student
16 usage and frequency, and participation in data sharing and training
17 necessary to maximize outcomes.
18 (c) Projections for the number of students who will be served,
19 broken down by partner district.
20 (d) A description of the efficacious nature of the eligible
21 tutoring partner's program, including the ability of the eligible
22 tutoring partner to hit certain benchmarks and goals, based on
23 evidence.
24 (e) A plan for how the eligible tutoring partner will target
25 high-impact tutoring for the highest need student populations based
26 on some or all of the following criteria:
27 (i) Statewide assessment data.
28 (ii) Screeners or formative assessment scores.
29 (iii) Economically disadvantaged status.

274
1 (iv) Special education status.
2 (v) English language learner status.
3 (f) A planned budget for the high-impact tutoring program
4 provided, including the total dollars necessary to serve the
5 identified student population and an estimate of the percentage of
6 awarded dollars that would be directly deployed for high-impact
7 tutoring.
8 (g) A commitment to provide periodic reporting to each
9 district served and to the department, in a form and manner
10 determined by the department, to ensure funds are being spent in
11 accordance with this section.
12 (h) A commitment to return any or all funds awarded if the
13 department determines dollars are used in a way that violates this
14 section.
15 (i) Any additional information required by the department to
16 make informed decisions on awarding competitive grants under this
17 section and to ensure financial stewardship awarded dollars.
18 (3) The department shall award grants to applicants on a
19 competitive basis, using at least all of the following in making
20 award determinations:
21 (a) The cost effectiveness of the application based on the
22 number of students projected to be served, the total requested
23 budget, and the demonstrated outcome capacity of the high-impact
24 tutoring program.
25 (b) The reasonability that the eligible tutoring partner will
26 be able to meet the stated outcome benchmarks.
27 (c) Other criteria, as determined by the department, to ensure
28 grants are awarded to the most impactful applicants.
29 (4) If the amount allocated in subsection (1) is not
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

275
1 sufficient to fully fund all eligible tutoring partners that apply
2 for funding, the department must prioritize grant awards to
3 districts with the lowest levels of literacy proficiency, as
4 determined by student scores on the Michigan student test of
5 educational progress (M-STEP).
6 (5) The department may approve applications from eligible
7 tutoring partners that offer innovative ways to reach students at a
8 lower cost by waiving elements of high-impact tutoring described in
9 subsection (11). This must include, but is not limited to, offering
10 virtual programs. In such cases, the eligible tutoring partner
11 submitting the application must provide evidence of program impact
12 and implement an outcomes-based contract model.
13 (6) It is the intent of the legislature that the work
14 associated with the grant application, oversight, and
15 administration of awards under this section be performed primarily
16 by the eligible tutoring partner and not by districts that have
17 agreed to receive high-impact tutoring.
18 (7) If a district or intermediate district agrees to receive
19 high-impact tutoring from an eligible tutoring partner, the
20 district or intermediate district must do all of the following:
21 (a) Work with the eligible tutoring partner to identify
22 students that would benefit from the program. The district or

25 outline possible student outcomes, and encourage student
26 participation.

28 program with the district's Michigan Integrated Continuous
29 Improvement Process (MICIP) plan.

11 (11) As used in this section:
12 (a) "Eligible tutoring partner" means a tutoring provider,
13 including, but not limited to, a tutoring provider created by for-
14 profit vendors, nonprofit vendors, intermediate districts,
15 districts, and the Michigan Schools for the Deaf and Blind, that
16 provides high-impact tutoring and meets at least 1 of the
17 following:
18 (i) Is included on the ProvenTutoring list maintained by the
19 Center for Research and Reform in Education at Johns Hopkins
20 University.
21 (ii) Has a Tutoring Program Design Badge as awarded by the
22 National Student Support Accelerator, a program of the SCALE
23 Initiative at Stanford University.
24 (iii) Is a vetted high-impact tutoring program included in the
25 MiStrategyBank. If necessary, the Michigan Association of
26 Intermediate School Administrators may convene a committee to
27 review tutoring programs for inclusion in the MiStrategyBank.
28 (b) "High-impact tutoring" means, except as otherwise provided
29 in this section, in-person tutoring that meets all of the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

279
1 following:
2 (i) Occurs before, during, or after the school day.
3 (ii) Is individualized, with tutor ratios not higher than 1:4.
4 (iii) Provides intensive support with a minimum of 2 sessions
5 per week.
6 (iv) Uses a structured, evidence-based curriculum designed for
7 tutoring, with formative assessments to track progress.
8 (v) Uses trained tutors.
9 (vi) Includes ongoing assessment and feedback.

8 Sec. 36. (1) From the state school aid fund money appropriated
9 in section 11, there is allocated for 2026-2027 only an amount not
10 to exceed $5,000,000.00 for the purposes described in this section,
11 including payments to improve educational outcomes in mathematics.
12 (2) The department shall create the committee for mathematics
13 achievement, which must include representatives of higher education
14 and mathematics educators who have expertise in mathematics
15 instruction and research. The committee shall exist until at least
16 September 2029. The department shall appoint the members of the
17 committee for mathematics achievement. The department shall develop
18 a process to identify and assemble experts for the committee for
19 mathematics achievement. The primary focus of the committee for
20 mathematics achievement is to recommend curricula and materials
21 with the goal of increasing student academic outcomes in the area
22 of mathematics. The committee for mathematics achievement is
23 encouraged to collaborate with entities with expertise on the
24 subject of mathematics. The department shall determine the size and
25 benchmarks of the committee for mathematics achievement. The
26 committee for mathematics achievement shall do all of the
27 following:
28 (a) Critically evaluate K to 5 mathematics curricula and
29 materials. The committee for mathematics achievement shall evaluate
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

281
1 with research-based outcomes, using the committee for mathematics
2 achievement's expertise in the field of mathematics. The committee
3 for mathematics achievement shall assign grade tiers to a
4 curriculum based on the curriculum's likelihood of increasing
5 student mathematics outcomes. Priority must be given to curricula
6 that already have a demonstrated history of increasing student
7 outcomes. The committee for mathematics achievement may perform an
8 evaluation under this subdivision based on submissions from a
9 vendor, but must independently verify the validity of information
10 provided by the vendor. The committee for mathematics achievement
11 is also strongly encouraged to evaluate available materials not
12 submitted to the department by a vendor.
13 (b) Create, maintain, and annually post on a publicly
14 available website a rankings list of all K to 5 mathematics
15 curricula the committee for mathematics achievement has evaluated.
16 This includes items from each part of the rankings list, items
17 evaluated in the 2026-2027 school year, and any subsequent items
18 evaluated going forward. The committee for mathematics achievement
19 shall denote on the list whether major changes have been made to a
20 mathematics curriculum on the list since the mathematics curriculum
21 was evaluated by the committee for mathematics achievement and if
22 the mathematics curriculum has not been evaluated since the last
23 ranking.
24 (c) The committee for mathematics achievement must select not
25 more than 5 mathematics curricula to recommend for purchase by
26 districts.
27 (3) An individual on the committee for mathematics achievement
28 shall recuse themselves from evaluating mathematics curricula or
29 other items that the individual has helped create, that the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

282
1 individual would benefit from financially, or for which the
2 individual has any other conflict of interest.
3 (4) From the funds allocated in subsection (1), and subject to
4 subsection (6), the department shall make payments to districts and
5 intermediate districts in an amount determined by the department.
6 The department shall not make payments under this subsection until
7 the committee for mathematics achievement has critically evaluated
8 as many mathematics curricula and other items from subsection (2)
9 as possible. Payments under this subsection may be used only to
10 purchase 1 of the 5 mathematics curricula described in subsection
11 (2)(c). If funding remains after all payments have been made to
12 districts and intermediate districts, the department may open a
13 second round of applications. The department shall award funding
14 under this subsection as a formula grant in an amount equal to the
15 amount necessary for the district or intermediate district to
16 implement the chosen mathematics curriculum or other item until
17 funding is depleted. The department may place a cap on the total
18 award per district or intermediate district.
19 (5) From the funds allocated in subsection (1), the department
20 may pay an annual stipend of up to $8,000.00 for each member of the
21 committee for mathematics achievement who is employed by or at a
22 public institution of higher education, a district, or an
23 intermediate district. A stipend paid under this subsection must be
24 commensurate with the committee member's contribution to the
25 committee for mathematics achievement. Funding must be paid to the
26 public institution of higher education, the district, or the
27 intermediate district where the committee member is employed, and
28 the public institution of higher education, the district, or the
29 intermediate district must then use funding received to award the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

283
1 stipend directly to the individual committee member. A committee
2 member who is not employed by or at a public institution of higher
3 education, a district, or an intermediate district is not eligible
4 to receive a stipend under this subsection.
5 (6) To be eligible for funding under subsection (4), a
6 district or intermediate district must apply in a form and manner
7 determined by the department. As a condition of receiving the
8 funding, the district or intermediate district must agree to
9 provide to the department information twice per year, on or before

15 required under subsection (2).

17 notwithstanding section 17b, the department shall make payments
18 under this section on a schedule determined by the department.

21 are carried forward into 2027-2028. The purpose of the work project
22 is to improve mathematics instructional practices. The estimated

1 Sec. 39. (1) An eligible applicant receiving funds under

4 and potential, by a date specified by the department of lifelong

6 fiscal year. An eligible applicant is not required to amend the

10 following:
11 (a) The estimated total number of age-eligible children in the
12 community, as provided to the applicant by the department of
13 lifelong education, advancement, and potential utilizing the most
14 recent population data available from the American Community Survey

16 lifelong education, advancement, and potential shall ensure that it
17 provides updated American Community Survey population data at least
18 once every 3 years.

20 community who are being served exclusively by Head Start programs
21 operating in the community.
22 (c) The number of children whom the applicant will have the
23 capacity to serve in each eligible great start readiness program
24 option who meet the age-eligible criteria of section 32d.
25 (2) The great start readiness target foundation amount for
26 2025-2026 2026-2027 is $10,650.00.$11,736.00.

12 expertise, and evidence-based instructional practices grounded in
13 the science of reading using the Michigan Multi-Tiered System of
14 Supports Technical Assistance Center. The department shall identify
15 an intermediate district to act as a fiscal agent for funds
16 allocated under this section.
17 (b) The department shall provide a report each year to the
18 state budget director, the house and senate appropriations

22 tiered systems of supports.
23 (ii) The progress being made by districts in providing reading
24 intervention services described in section 1280f of the revised
25 school code, MCL 380.1280f, for pupils in grades K to 12.
26 (iii) Other information the department determines would be
27 useful to understand the status of districts' implementation of
28 effective multi-tiered systems of supports and reading intervention
29 programs.

23 Sec. 62. (1) For the purposes of this section:
24 (a) "Membership" means for a particular fiscal year the total
25 membership of the intermediate district and the districts
26 constituent to the intermediate district or the total membership of
27 the area vocational-technical program, except that if a district
28 has elected not to come under sections 681 to 690 of the revised

CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

327
1 district are not included in the membership of the intermediate
2 district. However, the membership of a district that has elected
3 not to come under sections 681 to 690 of the revised school code,
4 MCL 380.681 to 380.690, is included in the membership of the
5 intermediate district if the district meets both of the following:
6 (i) The district operates the area vocational-technical
7 education program pursuant to a contract with the intermediate
8 district.
9 (ii) The district contributes an annual amount to the operation
10 of the program that is commensurate with the revenue that would
11 have been raised for operation of the program if millage were
12 levied in the district for the program under sections 681 to 690 of
13 the revised school code, MCL 380.681 to 380.690.
14 (b) "Millage levied" means the millage levied for area
15 vocational-technical education under sections 681 to 690 of the
16 revised school code, MCL 380.681 to 380.690, including a levy for
17 debt service obligations incurred as the result of borrowing for
18 capital outlay projects and in meeting capital projects fund
19 requirements of area vocational-technical education.

25 prepared and recommended those teachers for state certification.

28 parents, teachers, and school leaders on student achievement.

13 (2) The Michigan Virtual University shall operate the Michigan

11 (12) As used in this section:
12 (a) "Blended learning" means a hybrid instructional delivery
13 model where pupils are provided content, instruction, and
14 assessment, in part at a supervised educational facility away from
15 home where the pupil and a teacher with a valid Michigan teaching
16 certificate are in the same physical location and in part through
17 internet-connected learning environments with some degree of pupil
18 control over time, location, and pace of instruction.
19 (b) "Cyber school" means a full-time instructional program of
20 virtual courses for pupils that may or may not require attendance
21 at a physical school location.
22 (c) "Virtual course" means a course of study that is capable
23 of generating a credit or a grade and that is provided in an
24 interactive learning environment in which the majority of the
25 curriculum is delivered using the internet and in which pupils are
26 separated from their instructor or teacher of record by time or
27 location, or both.

16 section shall not work with the department to set prices or
17 policies for the program.
18 (10) As used in this section, "current fiscal year" means the
19 fiscal year for which an allocation is made under this section.
20 Sec. 99s. (1) From state school aid fund money appropriated
21 under section 11, there is allocated for 2026-2027 an amount not to
22 exceed $8,000,000.00 for Michigan science, technology, engineering,
23 and mathematics programs. Programs funded under this section are
24 intended to increase the number of pupils demonstrating proficiency
25 in science and mathematics on the state assessments, to increase
26 the number of pupils who are college- and career-ready upon high
27 school graduation, and to promote certificate and degree attainment
28 in STEM fields. Notwithstanding section 17b, the department shall
29 make payments under this section on a schedule determined by the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

366
1 department.
2 (2) The MiSTEM council annually shall review and make
3 recommendations to the governor, the legislature, and the
4 department concerning changes to the statewide strategy adopted by
5 the council for delivering STEM education-related opportunities to
6 pupils. The MiSTEM council shall use funds received under this
7 section to ensure that the MiSTEM council's members or their
8 designees are trained in a third-party evaluation system such as
9 the Change the Equation STEMworks program for the purpose of rating
10 STEM programs.
11 (3) The MiSTEM council shall make specific funding
12 recommendations for the funds allocated under subsection (4) by
13 December 15 of each fiscal year. Each specific funding
14 recommendation must be for a program or demonstration grant
15 approved by the MiSTEM council. All of the following apply:
16 (a) To be eligible for MiSTEM council approval as described in
17 this subsection, a program must satisfy all of the following:
18 (i) Align with this state's academic standards.
19 (ii) Have STEMworks or other third-party certification.
20 (iii) Provide project-based experiential learning, student
21 programming, or educator professional learning experiences.
22 (iv) Focus predominantly on classroom-based STEM experiences or
23 professional learning experiences.
24 (b) The MiSTEM council shall approve programs that represent
25 all network regions and include a diverse array of options for
26 students and educators.
27 (c) To be eligible for MiSTEM council approval as described in
28 this subsection, a demonstration grant must satisfy all of the
29 following:
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

367
1 (i) Align with this state's academic standards.
2 (ii) In collaboration with the department, scale project,
3 problem, or place-based learning in a building or district in
4 support of the statewide STEM strategy as recommended by the MiSTEM
5 council.
6 (d) The MiSTEM council is encouraged to work with the MiSTEM
7 network to develop locally and regionally developed programs,
8 demonstration grants, and professional learning experiences for the
9 programs on the list of approved programs. At the direction of the
10 MiSTEM council, funds can be used to evaluate and market regions,
11 programs, and demonstration grants.
12 (e) If the MiSTEM council is unable to make specific funding
13 recommendations by December 15 of a fiscal year, the department
14 shall award and distribute the funds allocated under subsection (4)
15 on a competitive grant basis that at least follows the statewide
16 STEM strategy plan and rating system recommended by the MiSTEM
17 council. Each grant must provide STEM education-related
18 opportunities for pupils.

23 recommended by the MiSTEM council.

1 region. The department shall distribute the remaining funds to each

5 (6) A MiSTEM network region shall do all of the following:
6 (a) Collaborate with the career and educational advisory
7 council that is located in the MiSTEM region and the department to
8 develop a regional strategic plan for STEM education that creates a
9 robust regional STEM culture, that empowers STEM teachers, that
10 integrates business and education into the STEM network, and that
11 ensures high-quality STEM experiences for pupils. At a minimum, a
12 regional STEM strategic plan described in this subdivision should
13 do all of the following in collaboration with the department:
14 (i) Identify regional employer need for STEM.
15 (ii) Identify processes for regional employers and educators to
16 create guided pathways for STEM careers that include internships or
17 externships, apprenticeships, and other experiential engagements
18 for pupils.
19 (iii) Identify educator professional learning opportunities,
20 including internships or externships and apprenticeships, that
21 integrate this state's science standards into high-quality STEM
22 experiences that engage pupils.
23 (b) Facilitate regional STEM events such as educator and
24 employer networking and STEM career fairs to raise STEM awareness.
25 (c) Contribute to the MiSTEM website and engage in other
26 MiSTEM network functions to further the mission of STEM in this
27 state in coordination with the MiSTEM council and the department of
28 labor and economic opportunity.
29 (d) Facilitate application and implementation of state and
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

369
1 federal funds under this subsection and any other grants or funds
2 for the MiSTEM network region.
3 (e) Work with districts to provide STEM programming and
4 professional learning.
5 (f) Coordinate recurring discussions and work with the career
6 and educational advisory council to ensure that feedback and best
7 practices are being shared, including funding, program,
8 professional learning opportunities, and regional strategic plans.
9 (g) In collaboration with the department, deploy marketing and
10 outreach efforts to publish the availability of STEM learning
11 opportunities.
12 (7) To receive funds under this section, a grant recipient

17 (8) To receive funds under this section, a grant recipient

19 private resources for the funds received under this subsection.
20 (9) By not later than July 1 of each fiscal year for which

25 the executive director. The performance measures must be designed
26 to ensure that the activities of the MiSTEM network are improving

29 (2) A virtual reality simulation pilot program described in
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

371
1 this section must do all of the following:
2 (a) Offer tactile, multimodal learning experiences that teach
3 core state standards-aligned concepts and skills for grades 6 to 12
4 mathematics and science, with a focus on improving outcomes on
5 standards-aligned algebra I and middle school math benchmarks.
6 (b) Immerse students in real-world problems across occupations
7 and career clusters to build career exposure and relevance, using
8 3-D models and interactives that visualize and manipulate abstract
9 mathematical and science concepts. A virtual reality program
10 provider described in this section must have already conducted a
11 randomized controlled trial by a third-party evaluator that
12 demonstrated double-digit growth outcomes in algebra I benchmark
13 assessments.

15 occupations across career clusters aligned with local and market
16 needs. Districts participating in a pilot program described in this
17 section shall provide access to all mathematics and science
18 teachers in a district for the purpose of building the strong
19 quantitative and critical thinking foundations that every student
20 requires to be successful across career clusters.

8 Sec. 101. (1) To be eligible to receive state aid under this
9 article, not later than the fifth Wednesday after the pupil
10 membership count day and not later than the fifth Wednesday after
11 the supplemental count day, each district superintendent shall
12 submit and certify to the center and the intermediate
13 superintendent, in the form and manner prescribed by the center,
14 the number of pupils enrolled and in regular daily attendance,
15 including identification of tuition-paying pupils, in the district
16 as of the pupil membership count day and as of the supplemental
17 count day, as applicable, for the current school year. In addition,
18 a district maintaining school during the entire year shall submit
19 and certify to the center and the intermediate superintendent, in
20 the form and manner prescribed by the center, the number of pupils
21 enrolled and in regular daily attendance in the district for the
22 current school year pursuant to rules promulgated by the
23 superintendent. Not later than the sixth Wednesday after the pupil
24 membership count day and not later than the sixth Wednesday after
25 the supplemental count day, the district shall resolve any pupil
26 membership conflicts with another district, correct any data
27 issues, and recertify the data in a form and manner prescribed by
28 the center and file the certified data with the intermediate
29 superintendent. If a district fails to submit and certify the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

373
1 attendance data, as required under this subsection, the center
2 shall notify the department and the department shall withhold state
3 aid due to be distributed under this article from the defaulting
4 district immediately, beginning with the next payment after the
5 failure and continuing with each payment until the district
6 complies with this subsection. If a district does not comply with
7 this subsection by the end of the fiscal year, the district
8 forfeits the amount withheld. A person who willfully falsifies a
9 figure or statement in the certified and sworn copy of enrollment
10 is subject to penalty as prescribed by section 161.
11 (2) To be eligible to receive state aid under this article,
12 not later than the twenty-fourth Wednesday after the pupil
13 membership count day and not later than the twenty-fourth Wednesday

21 year, the superintendent shall waive for a district the minimum
22 number of hours and days of pupil instruction requirement of
23 subsection (3) for a department-approved alternative education
24 program or another innovative program approved by the department,
25 including a 4-day school week. If a district applies for and

27 of the waiver, the district is not subject to forfeiture under this
28 section for the specific program covered by the waiver. If the
29 district does not comply with the terms of the waiver, the amount

385
1 of the forfeiture is calculated based upon a comparison of the
2 number of hours and days of pupil instruction actually provided to
3 the minimum number of hours and days of pupil instruction required
4 under subsection (3). A district shall report pupils enrolled in a
5 department-approved alternative education program under this

8 subsection:
9 (a) If the waiver is for a blended model of delivery, a waiver
10 that is granted for the 2011-2012 fiscal year or a subsequent
11 fiscal year remains in effect unless it is revoked by the
12 superintendent.
13 (b) If the waiver is for a 100% online model of delivery and
14 the educational program for which the waiver is granted makes
15 educational services available to pupils for a minimum of at least
16 1,098 hours during a school year and ensures that each pupil is on
17 track for course completion at proficiency level, a waiver that is
18 granted for the 2011-2012 fiscal year or a subsequent fiscal year
19 remains in effect unless it is revoked by the superintendent.
20 (c) A waiver that is not a waiver described in subdivision (a)
21 or (b) is valid for 3 fiscal years, unless it is revoked by the
22 superintendent, and must be renewed at the end of the 3-year period
23 to remain in effect.
24 (10) A district may count up to 38 hours of professional
25 development for teachers as hours of pupil instruction. All of the
26 following apply to the counting of professional development as
27 pupil instruction under this subsection:
28 (a) If the professional development exceeds 5 hours in a
29 single day, that day may be counted as a day of pupil instruction.
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

386
1 (b) At least 8 hours of the professional development counted
2 as hours of pupil instruction under this subsection must be
3 recommended by a districtwide professional development advisory
4 committee appointed by the district board. The advisory committee
5 must be composed of teachers employed by the district who represent
6 a variety of grades and subject matter specializations, including
7 special education; nonteaching staff; parents; and administrators.
8 The majority membership of the committee must be composed of
9 teaching staff.
10 (c) Professional development provided online is allowable and
11 encouraged, as long as the instruction has been approved by the
12 district. The department shall issue a list of approved online
13 professional development providers that must include the Michigan
14 Virtual School.
15 (d) Professional development may only be counted as hours of
16 pupil instruction under this subsection for the pupils of those
17 teachers scheduled to participate in the professional development.
18 (e) The professional development must meet all of the
19 following to be counted as pupil instruction under this subsection:
20 (i) Be aligned to the school or district improvement plan for
21 the school or district in which the professional development is
22 being provided.
23 (ii) Be linked to 1 or more criteria in the evaluation tool
24 developed or adopted by the district or intermediate district under
25 section 1249 of the revised school code, MCL 380.1249.
26 (iii) Has been approved by the department as counting for state
27 continuing education clock hours. The number of hours of
28 professional development counted as hours of pupil instruction
29 under this subsection may not exceed the number of state continuing
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

387
1 education clock hours for which the professional development was
2 approved.
3 (iv) Not more than a combined total of 10 hours of the
4 professional development takes place before the first scheduled day
5 of school for the school year ending in the fiscal year and after
6 the last scheduled day of school for that school year.
7 (v) Not more than 10 hours of the professional development
8 takes place in a single month.
9 (vi) At least 75% of teachers scheduled to participate in the
10 professional development are in attendance.
11 (11) Subsections (3) and (8) do not apply to a school of

16 enrollment in an eligible career and technical skills program under
17 subsection (15). Up to 10% of adult education participants served
18 under subsection (15) may already have a high school diploma or a
19 high school equivalency certificate at the time of enrollment in an
20 eligible career and technical skills program under subsection (15)
21 and receive remediation services. It is intended that the cap
22 described in the immediately preceding sentence is continually
23 lowered on an annual basis until it eventually is 0%.
24 (18) The department shall approve at least 2 high school
25 equivalency tests and determine whether a high school equivalency
26 certificate meets the requisite standards for high school
27 equivalency in this state.

1 advisory council to the local workforce development boards located

4 (b) "Career pathway" means a combination of rigorous and high-
5 quality education, training, and other services that comply with
6 all of the following:
7 (i) Aligns with the skill needs of industries in the economy of
8 this state or in the regional economy involved.
9 (ii) Prepares an individual to be successful in any of a full
10 range of secondary or postsecondary education options, including
11 apprenticeships registered under the act of August 16, 1937,
12 commonly referred to as the national apprenticeship act, 29 USC 50
13 et seq.

15 the individual's education and career goals.
16 (iv) Includes, as appropriate, education offered concurrently
17 with and in the same context as workforce preparation activities
18 and training for a specific occupation or occupational cluster.
19 (v) Organizes education, training, and other services to meet
20 the particular needs of an individual in a manner that accelerates
21 the educational and career advancement of the individual to the
22 extent practicable.
23 (vi) Enables an individual to attain a secondary school diploma
24 or its recognized equivalent, and at least 1 recognized
25 postsecondary credential.
26 (vii) Helps an individual enter or advance within a specific
27 occupation or occupational cluster.

22 Sec. 163. (1) Except as otherwise provided in the revised
23 school code or rules promulgated by the department, the board of a
24 district or intermediate district shall not permit any of the

17 Sec. 164k. (1) If the department receives a report that a
18 district or intermediate district is violating any of the following
19 requirements, and the department confirms the violation, the
20 department shall withhold 5% of the payment the district is
21 eligible to receive under section 22b, or 5% of the payment the
22 intermediate district is eligible to receive under section 81, as
23 applicable, for as long as the district or intermediate district is
24 out of compliance:
25 (a) A district or intermediate district shall ensure that all
26 food made available to a student in the breakfast or lunch program
27 complies with all federal rules and regulations related to school
28 meals, including provisions restricting access to soda and candy as
29 described in federal Smart Snacks Standards.

427
1 (b) To the extent practicable under federal regulations, a
2 district or intermediate district shall require each student
3 household to complete the child nutrition and education benefits
4 application, as provided by the department, for free and reduced-
5 price school meals regardless of whether the district or
6 intermediate district opts to provide universal breakfast or lunch.
7 (c) For pupil membership count days after the fall 2025 count,
8 a district or intermediate district shall not provide a financial
9 incentive to students for attending pupil membership count day.
10 (d) A district or intermediate district shall ensure that
11 student survey questions and results are made available to the
12 public, posted on the district's or intermediate district's
13 website, and that parents and legal guardians are notified of the
14 survey. As used in this subsection, "survey" includes any survey
15 from the district or intermediate district or from the local,
16 state, or federal government.
17 (e) Beginning with the fiscal year ending September 30, 2026,
18 if a district is not using a curriculum from the department's

20 revised school code, MCL 380.1280f, the district must provide a
21 notification to all parents or legal guardians of students in

23 includes all of the following:
24 (i) A statement informing parents or legal guardians that the

26 aligned to state standards, which could negatively impact student

428

28 Sec. 164m. (1) Except as otherwise provided in section
29 22b(3)(g), a district that receives funds under section 22b(4) must
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

434
1 do all of the following:
2 (a) Use funds received for purposes including, but not limited
3 to, student achievement and success, literacy, mathematics, and
4 providing direct English language development instruction.
5 (b) Administer to English language learners the English
6 language proficiency assessment known as the "WIDA ACCESS for
7 English language learners" or the "WIDA Alternate ACCESS".
8 (c) Agree to meet or exceed the minimum number of minutes per
9 week, as determined by the department under subsection (2), that
10 the district provides direct English language development
11 instruction.
12 (d) Implement a multi-tiered system of supports that is an
13 evidence-based framework that uses data-driven problem solving to
14 integrate academic and behavioral instruction and that uses
15 intervention delivered to all pupils in varying intensities based
16 on pupil needs. The multi-tiered system of supports must be
17 implemented in grades K to 12 or, if the district does not operate
18 all of grades K to 12, for all of the grades the district operates.
19 The multi-tiered system of supports described in this subdivision
20 must provide at least all of the following essential components:
21 (i) Team-based leadership.
22 (ii) A tiered delivery system.
23 (iii) Selection and implementation of instruction,
24 interventions, and supports.
25 (iv) A comprehensive screening and assessment system.
26 (v) Continuous data-based decision making.
27 (e) Support pupil needs for pupils counted toward the
28 district's weighted pupil membership, including, but not limited
29 to, items identified in a district's Michigan Integrated Continuous

9 Sec. 164o. Districts and intermediate districts are encouraged
10 to evaluate and pursue efficiency and cost-containment measures
11 that maximize state funding. Districts and intermediate districts
12 shall identify practices that increase efficiencies, including, but
13 not limited to, establishing joint ventures, consolidating
14 services, utilizing program collaborations, maximizing educational
15 benefits through optimal class sizes and frequency of course
16 offerings, eliminating low-enrollment and high-cost instructional
17 programs, using self-insurance, practicing energy conservation, and
18 utilizing group purchasing.

4 Sec. 201i. (1) Not later than Within 30 days after of the
5 enactment of the an amendatory act that added this section, to this
6 article, the legislature house and senate shall provide to the
7 responsible entity and the state budget director office a jointly
8 agreed upon list of legislatively directed spending items , which
9 may be referred to in this section as grants or direct
10 appropriation grants, funded under the amendatory act that added
11 this section consistent with house or senate rules and this
12 section. funded by the amendatory act. The list must include all
13 information and documents pertaining to the funded items as
14 publicly disclosed in accordance with house or senate rules and
15 this section. sections 364 and 364a of the management and budget
16 act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and 18.1364a. As used in this
17 subsection, "legislatively directed spending item" means that term
18 as defined in section 364 of the management and budget act, 1984 PA
19 431, MCL 18.1364.
20 (2) Notwithstanding any other conditions or requirements for
21 direct appropriation grants, the responsible entity shall perform,
22 at a minimum, at least all of the following activities to
23 administer the grants described in subsection (1):
24 (a) Establish a process to review, complete, and execute a
25 grant agreement with a grant recipient. The responsible entity
26 shall not execute a grant agreement unless all necessary
27 documentation has been submitted and reviewed.
28 (b) Verify to the extent possible that a grant recipient is a
29 not-for-profit entity and will use funds as publicly disclosed and
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

448
1 for a public purpose that serves the economic prosperity, health,
2 safety, or general welfare of the residents of this state.
3 (c) Review and verify all necessary information to ensure the
4 grant recipient is reasonably able to execute the grant agreement,
5 perform its fiduciary duty, and comply with all applicable state
6 and federal statutes. The responsible entity may deduct the cost of
7 background checks and any other efforts performed as part of this
8 verification from the amount of the designated grant award.
9 (d) Disburse the grant money per the grant disbursement
10 schedule in the executed grant agreement on a reimbursement basis
11 after the grantee has provided sufficient documentation, as
12 determined by the responsible entity, to verify that expenditures
13 were made in accordance with the project purpose.
14 (e) If the state budget director determines that information
15 provided by the grantee does not meet the disclosure requirements,
16 that the grant will be used to pay a tax lien, delinquent tax, or
17 other obligation owed to this state, or that the grant will create
18 a conflict of interest, the responsible entity shall not release
19 the grant money to the grantee. Money that is not released under
20 this subdivision lapses at the end of the fiscal year. There is not
21 a conflict of interest if the sponsoring legislator certifies that
22 the sponsoring legislator's immediate family members, legislative
23 staff members that have worked for the sponsoring legislator within
24 the past 2 years, and the sponsoring legislator do not have a
25 direct or indirect pecuniary interest in the legislatively directed
26 spending item.
27 (3) An executed grant agreement under this section between the
28 responsible entity and a grant recipient must include at least all
29 of the following:

449
1 (a) All necessary identifying information for the grant
2 recipient, including any tax and financial information necessary

7 budget. Project budget must include how all grant money will be

3 Sec. 202a. As used in this article:
4 (a) "Center" means the center for educational performance and
5 information created in section 94a.
6 (b) "College level equivalent credit examination" means an
7 examination that is administered by an independent testing service
8 and that is used by colleges and universities generally to award
9 postsecondary credit for achievement of a particular score, and
10 includes, but is not limited to, advanced placement examinations,
11 the DANTES Subject Standardized Test (DSST), and college-level
12 examination program (CLEP) examinations.
13 (c) "Participating college" means a community college that is
14 a reporting unit of the retirement system and that reports
15 employees to the retirement system for the state fiscal year.
16 (d) "Retirement system" means the Michigan public school
17 employees' retirement system under the public school employees
18 retirement act of 1979, 1980 PA 300, MCL 38.1301 to 38.1437.
19 (e) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
20 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
21 subcommittees on community colleges, the senate and house fiscal
22 agencies, the senate and house policy offices, and the state budget
23 office.
24 Sec. 203. (1) Unless otherwise specified, a community college
25 that receives appropriations in section 201 and the center shall
26 use the internet to fulfill the reporting requirements of this
27 article. This requirement includes transmission of reports via
28 electronic mail to the recipients identified for each reporting
29 requirement and placement of reports each required report in a
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

453
1 single archivable location on an internet site, not later than the
2 due date required for each report.
3 (2) In addition to placing all required reports in the current
4 fiscal year on the community college's website, a community college
5 shall maintain on its website all reports placed on the website
6 from previous 7 fiscal years, posted by fiscal year in the same
7 single archivable location. This requirement does not apply to the
8 center.
9 Sec. 205. To the extent possible, the principal executive
10 officer of each community college that receives appropriations in
11 section 201 shall take all reasonable steps to ensure that
12 geographically disadvantaged business enterprises compete for and
13 perform contracts to provide services or supplies, or both. Each
14 principal executive officer shall strongly encourage businesses
15 with which the community college contracts to subcontract with
16 certified geographically disadvantaged business enterprises for
17 services or supplies, or both. As used in this section,
18 "geographically disadvantaged business enterprises" means that term
19 as defined in Executive Directive No. 2023-1.2019-8.

20 department of treasury certifies to the state budget director that

24 Sec. 210b. By March 1 of each year, the Michigan Community

26 Universities shall submit a report to the senate and house
27 appropriations subcommittees on community colleges, the senate and

455
1 the state budget director on the activities and programs focused on
2 improving transfer student outcomes since March 1 of the previous
3 year, including all of the following:
4 (a) The direct transferability of mathematics gateway courses
5 between and among community colleges and universities.
6 (b) The implementation of MiTransfer pathways.
7 (c) The progress on increasing participation in MiTransfer
8 pathways among community colleges and public universities.
9 (d) The implementation of the Michigan Transfer Network at
10 mitransfer.org.
11 (e) A progress report on the implementation of the Michigan
12 transfer agreement.
13 Sec. 212. Community college districts are encouraged to
14 evaluate and pursue efficiency and cost-containment measures that
15 maximize state funding. Community colleges shall identify practices
16 that increase efficiencies, including, but not limited to,
17 establishing joint ventures, consolidating services, utilizing
18 program collaborations, maximizing educational benefits through
19 optimal class sizes and frequency of course offerings, increasing
20 web-based instruction, eliminating low-enrollment and high-cost
21 instructional programs, using self-insurance, practicing energy
22 conservation, and utilizing group purchasing. Community colleges
23 shall also review proposed capital outlay projects to increase
24 coordination and utilization of new facilities, renovation
25 projects, and technology improvements.

1 recipients. The report must include all of the following:
2 (a) A list of all work project accounts.

27 Sec. 217b. (1) Each community college that receives an
28 appropriation in section 201 shall report to the center by the last
29 business day of August of each year the tuition and mandatory fees
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

461
1 paid by a full-time in-district student and a full-time out-of-
2 district student as established by the community college governing
3 board for the current academic year. This report should also
4 include the annual cost of tuition and fees based on a full-time
5 course load of 30 credits. This report must also specify the amount
6 that tuition and fees have increased for the community college from
7 the prior academic year. Each community college shall also report
8 any revisions to the reported current academic year tuition and
9 mandatory fees adopted by the community college governing board to
10 the center within 15 days of being adopted. The center shall
11 provide this information and any revisions to the house and senate
12 fiscal agencies and the state budget director.standard report
13 recipients.
14 (2) Each community college that receives an appropriation in
15 section 201 shall certify to the state budget director by the first
16 last business day of November August of each year that its board
17 will not adopt an increase in tuition and fee rates for in-district
18 students for the academic year that is greater than the tuition
19 restraint described in this subsection. For the academic year 2025-
20 2026, the tuition restraint level is equal to the greater of 4.5%
21 or $227.00. For the academic year 2026-2027, the tuition restraint
22 level is equal to the greater of 4.0% or $199.00. For the academic
23 year 2027-2028, the tuition restraint level is equal to the greater
24 of 4.0% or $199.00. It is intended that in the next fiscal year,
25 the tuition restraint rate will be adjusted only for the subsequent
26 academic year. As used in this subsection:
27 (a) "Fee" means any board-authorized fee that will be paid by
28 more than 1/2 of all in-district students at least once during
29 their enrollment at a community college. A community college
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

462
1 increasing a fee that applies to a specific subset of students or
2 courses shall provide sufficient information to prove that the
3 increase applied to that subset will not cause the increase in the
4 average amount of board-authorized total tuition and fees paid by
5 in-district students in the academic year to exceed the limit
6 established in this section.
7 (b) "Tuition and fee rate" means the average of full-time
8 rates paid by a majority of students in each class, based on an
9 unweighted average of the rates authorized by the community college
10 board and actually charged to students, deducting any uniformly
11 rebated or refunded amounts, for the 2 semesters with the highest
12 levels of full-time equated in-district enrollment during the
13 academic year.
14 (3) Community colleges that exceed the tuition and fee rate
15 cap described in subsection (2) are not eligible to receive
16 payments under section 201f for 1-time performance funding payments
17 for fiscal year 2025-2026. 2026-2027. The state budget director
18 shall implement uniform reporting requirements to ensure that a
19 community college receiving a payment under section 201f for 1-time
20 performance funding has satisfied the tuition restraint
21 requirements of this section. The state budget director has the
22 sole authority to determine if a community college has met the
23 requirements of this section. Information reported by a community
24 college to the state budget director under this subsection must
25 also be reported to the house and senate appropriations
26 subcommittees on community colleges and the house and senate fiscal
27 agencies.standard report recipients.
28 (4) Notwithstanding any other provision of this act, the
29 legislature may at any time adjust appropriations for a community
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

463
1 college that adopts an increase in tuition and fee rates for in-
2 district students that exceeds the rate cap established in
3 subsection (2).

472
1 (3) For purposes of subsection (2), the following categories
2 of best practices reflect functional activities of community
3 colleges that have strategic value to the local communities and
4 regional economies:
5 (a) For Category A, economic development and business or
6 industry partnerships, the following:
7 (i) The community college has active partnerships with local
8 employers including hospitals and health care providers.
9 (ii) The community college provides customized on-site training
10 for area companies, employees, or both.
11 (iii) The community college supports entrepreneurship through a
12 small business assistance center or other training or consulting
13 activities targeted toward small businesses.
14 (iv) The community college supports technological advancement
15 through industry partnerships, incubation activities, or operation
16 of a Michigan technical education center or other advanced
17 technology center.
18 (v) The community college has active partnerships with local
19 or regional workforce and economic development agencies.
20 (b) For Category B, educational partnerships, the following:
21 (i) The community college has active partnerships with regional
22 high schools, intermediate school districts, and career-tech
23 centers to provide instruction through dual enrollment, concurrent
24 enrollment, direct credit, middle college, or academy programs.
25 (ii) The community college hosts, sponsors, or participates in
26 enrichment programs for area K-12 students, such as college days,
27 summer or after-school programming, or Science Olympiad.
28 (iii) The community college provides, supports, or participates
29 in programming to promote successful transitions to college for
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

473
1 traditional age students, including grant programs such as talent
2 search, upward bound, or other activities to promote college
3 readiness in area high schools and community centers.
4 (iv) The community college provides, supports, or participates
5 in programming to promote successful transitions to college for new
6 or reentering adult students, such as adult basic education, a high
7 school equivalency test preparation program and testing, or
8 recruiting, advising, or orientation activities specific to adults.
9 As used in this subparagraph, "high school equivalency test
10 preparation program" means that term as defined in section 4.
11 (v) The community college has active partnerships with
12 regional 4-year colleges and universities to promote successful
13 transfer, such as articulation, 2+2, or reverse transfer agreements
14 or operation of a university center.
15 (c) For Category C, community services, the following:
16 (i) The community college provides continuing education
17 programming for leisure, wellness, personal enrichment, or
18 professional development.
19 (ii) The community college operates or sponsors opportunities
20 for community members to engage in activities that promote leisure,
21 wellness, cultural or personal enrichment such as community sports
22 teams, theater or musical ensembles, or artist guilds.
23 (iii) The community college operates public facilities to
24 promote cultural, educational, or personal enrichment for community
25 members, such as libraries, computer labs, performing arts centers,
26 museums, art galleries, or television or radio stations.

1 centers, hiking or biking trails, or natural areas.

4 members.

6 1-time performance funding payments under section 201f must be made
7 to a community college only if that community college actively
8 participates in the Michigan Transfer Network sponsored by the
9 Michigan Association of Collegiate Registrars and Admissions
10 Officers and submits timely updates, including updated course
11 equivalencies at least every 6 months, to the Michigan Transfer
12 Network. The state budget director shall determine if a community
13 college has not satisfied this requirement. The state budget
14 director may withhold payments for performance funding under
15 section 201 and 1-time performance funding under section 201f until

24 Sec. 236j. (1) The postsecondary scholarship fund is created
25 in the department of treasury for the purpose of providing
26 scholarship awards to eligible students who attend eligible
27 postsecondary educational institutions in this state, as provided
28 in subsection (5).
29 (2) The state treasurer may receive money or other assets from
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

483
1 any source for deposit into the postsecondary scholarship fund. The
2 state treasurer shall direct the investment of the postsecondary
3 scholarship fund. The state treasurer shall credit to the
4 postsecondary scholarship fund interest and earnings from
5 postsecondary scholarship fund investments.
6 (3) Money in the postsecondary scholarship fund at the close
7 of the fiscal year must remain in the postsecondary scholarship
8 fund and not lapse to the general fund.
9 (4) The department of treasury is the administrator of the
10 postsecondary scholarship fund for auditing purposes.
11 (5) Money must be expended from the postsecondary scholarship
12 fund only for the purpose of providing Michigan achievement
13 scholarship awards to eligible students who attend eligible
14 postsecondary educational institutions in this state and for other
15 purposes described in this section. From the funds appropriated in
16 section 236(7) for the Michigan achievement scholarship, the
17 department of lifelong education, advancement, and potential may
18 use up to $10,000,000.00 annually for the purposes of outreach
19 programs to raise awareness of the Michigan achievement scholarship
20 and other state scholarship programs allocated in section 236(7).

22 shall ensure that state scholarships are well publicized and that
23 high school students are provided information on the availability
24 of financial aid. The department of lifelong education,
25 advancement, and potential may receive and expend funds received

28 education, advancement, and potential shall provide the necessary

1 431, MCL 18.1364.
2 (2) Notwithstanding any other conditions or requirements for
3 direct appropriation grants, the responsible entity shall perform,
4 at a minimum, at least all of the following activities to
5 administer the grants described in subsection (1):
6 (a) Establish a process to review, complete, and execute a
7 grant agreement with a grant recipient. The responsible entity
8 shall not execute a grant agreement unless all necessary
9 documentation has been submitted and reviewed.
10 (b) Verify to the extent possible that a grant recipient is a
11 not-for-profit entity and will use funds as publicly disclosed and
12 for a public purpose that serves the economic prosperity, health,
13 safety, or general welfare of the residents of this state.
14 (c) Review and verify all necessary information to ensure the
15 grant recipient is reasonably able to execute the grant agreement,
16 perform its fiduciary duty, and comply with all applicable state
17 and federal statutes. The responsible entity may deduct the cost of
18 background checks and any other efforts performed as part of this
19 verification from the amount of the designated grant award.
20 (d) Disburse the grant money per the grant disbursement
21 schedule in the executed grant agreement on a reimbursement basis
22 after the grantee has provided sufficient documentation, as
23 determined by the responsible entity, to verify that expenditures
24 were made in accordance with the project purpose.
25 (e) If the state budget director determines that information
26 provided by the grantee does not meet the disclosure requirements,
27 that the grant will be used to pay a tax lien, delinquent tax, or
28 other obligation owed to this state, or that the grant will create
29 a conflict of interest, the responsible entity shall not release
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

488
1 the grant money to the grantee. Money that is not released under
2 this subdivision lapses at the end of the fiscal year. There is not
3 a conflict of interest if the sponsoring legislator certifies that
4 the sponsoring legislator's immediate family members, legislative
5 staff members that have worked for the sponsoring legislator within
6 the past 2 years, and the sponsoring legislator do not have a
7 direct or indirect pecuniary interest in the legislatively directed
8 spending item.
9 (3) An executed grant agreement under this section between the
10 responsible entity and a grant recipient must include at least all
11 of the following:
12 (a) All necessary identifying information for the grant
13 recipient, including any tax and financial information necessary
14 for the responsible entity to administer grant money under this
15 section.
16 (b) A description of the project for which the grant money
17 will be expended, including tentative timelines and the estimated
18 budget. Project budget must include how all grant money will be
19 used and must indicate if any grant money will be provided to a
20 third party or subrecipient. The responsible entity shall not
21 reimburse expenditures that are outside of the project purpose, as
22 stated in the executed grant agreement, from appropriations under
23 the amendatory act that added this section. The grantee shall
24 return to the state treasury any interest in excess of $1,000.00
25 earned on the grant money while unexpended and in possession of the
26 grantee.

5 was misused or that use of the money was misrepresented by the
6 grant recipient, the responsible entity shall not award any
7 additional funds under the executed grant agreement and shall refer
8 the grant for review following internal audit protocols, which may
9 include referral for criminal investigation.
10 (11) As used in this section, "responsible entity" means the
11 department of lifelong education, advancement, and potential, a
12 public university, or other person that administers a grant under
13 this article.
14 Sec. 237b. As used in this article:
15 (a) "Center" means the center for educational performance and
16 information created in section 94a.
17 (b) "College level equivalent credit examination" means an
18 examination that is administered by an independent testing service
19 and that is used by colleges and universities generally to award
20 postsecondary credit for achievement of a particular score, and
21 includes, but is not limited to, advanced placement examinations,
22 the DANTES Subject Standardized Test (DSST), and college-level
23 examination program (CLEP) examinations.
24 (c) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
25 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
26 subcommittees on higher education, the senate and house fiscal
27 agencies, the senate and house policy offices, and the state budget
28 office.
