28 funding. Public universities shall identify practices that increase
29 efficiencies, including, but not limited to, establishing joint
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

493
1 ventures, consolidating services, utilizing program collaborations,
2 maximizing educational benefits through optimal class sizes and
3 frequency of course offerings, eliminating low-enrollment and high-
4 cost instructional programs, using self-insurance, practicing
5 energy conservation, and utilizing group purchasing. Public
6 universities shall also review proposed capital outlay projects to
7 increase coordination and utilization of new facilities, renovation
8 projects, and technology improvements.

7 (a) Maintain residency in this state, as determined for
8 purposes of the Free Application for Federal Student Aid.
9 (b) Have graduated from high school in this state with a high
10 school diploma or certificate of completion or achieved a high
11 school equivalency certificate in 2023 or after.
12 (c) Be enrolled in an eligible institution as a full-time
13 undergraduate student, at an eligible institution, as defined by
14 that eligible institution, and be a first-time enrollee for the
15 semester the award is received.
16 (d) Have enrolled for the first time in an eligible
17 institution during the 2023-2024 academic year, or a subsequent
18 academic year, within 15 months after high school graduation or
19 attainment of a high school equivalency certificate or have
20 received a Michigan achievement scholarship or Michigan achievement
21 skills scholarship in a previous academic year. For the purposes of
22 this subdivision, participation in a dual enrollment, early
23 college, or other similar program while attending high school does
24 not disqualify a student from being considered a first-time
25 enrollee.
26 (e) (d) Maintain satisfactory academic progress, as defined by
27 the eligible institution in which the student is enrolled.
28 (f) (e) Not be in default on a federal student loan.
29 (g) (f) Apply for all available gift aid for each academic

1 section and pursuant to the administrative procedures for the

4 (2) As used in this section:
5 (a) "Phase I" means the first part of the tuition incentive
6 program defined as the academic period of 80 semester or 120 term
7 credits, or less, leading to an associate degree or certificate.
8 Students must be enrolled in a certificate or associate degree
9 program and taking classes within the program of study for a
10 certificate or associate degree. Tuition will not be covered for
11 courses outside of a certificate or associate degree program.
12 (b) "Phase II" means the second part of the tuition incentive
13 program that provides assistance in the third and fourth year of 4-
14 year degree programs.
15 (c) "Department" means the department of lifelong education,
16 advancement, and potential.
17 (d) "High school equivalency certificate" means that term as
18 defined in section 4.
19 (3) An individual must meet the following basic criteria and
20 financial thresholds to be eligible for tuition incentive program
21 benefits:
22 (a) To be eligible for phase I, an individual must meet all of
23 the following criteria:
24 (i) Be less than 20 years of age at the time the individual
25 graduates from high school with a diploma or certificate of
26 completion or achieves a high school equivalency certificate or,
27 for students attending a 5-year middle college approved by the
28 Michigan department of education, be less than 21 years of age when
29 the individual graduates from high school, or be considered a

524
1 special education pupil at the time the individual graduated from
2 high school as described in section 6(4)(l)(i).

4 state, as determined for purposes of the Free Application for
5 Federal Student Aid.
6 (iii) Be at least a half-time student, earning less than 80
7 semester or 120 term credits at a participating educational
8 institution within 4 years of high school graduation or achievement
9 of a high school equivalency certificate. All program eligibility
10 expires 10 years after initial enrollment at a participating
11 educational institution.
12 (iv) Meet the satisfactory academic progress policy of the
13 educational institution attended by the individual.
14 (b) To be eligible for phase II, an individual must meet
15 either of the following criteria in addition to the criteria in
16 subdivision (a):
17 (i) Complete at least 56 transferable semester or 84
18 transferable term credits.
19 (ii) Obtain an associate degree or certificate at a
20 participating institution.
21 (c) To be eligible for phase I or phase II, an individual must
22 be financially eligible as determined by the department. An
23 individual is financially eligible for the tuition incentive
24 program if the individual was eligible for Medicaid from this state
25 for 24 months within any 36 months prior to completion of high
26 school or achievement of a high school equivalency certificate. The

525
1 any 36 months prior to completion of high school or achievement of

5 behalf of a person eligible under subsection (3). The department
6 shall only accept standard per-credit hour tuition billings and
7 shall reject billings that are excessive or outside the guidelines
8 for the type of educational institution.
9 (5) For phase I, all of the following apply:
10 (a) Payments for associate degree or certificate programs must
11 not be made for more than 80 semester or 120 term credits for any
12 individual student at any participating institution. The department
13 shall not do either of the following:

18 for a student whose semester-credit or term-credit load will not

20 maximum provided in this subdivision.

18 subcommittees on higher education, and the house and senate fiscal
19 agencies standard report recipients a report on North American
20 Indian tuition waivers for the preceding academic year that
21 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following information:
22 (a) The number of waiver applications received and the number
23 of waiver applications approved.
24 (b) For each public university submitting information under
25 subsection (3), all of the following:
26 (i) The number of graduate and undergraduate North American
27 Indian Native American students enrolled each term for the previous
28 academic year.
29 (ii) The number of North American Indian tuition waivers
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

530
1 granted each term, including to continuing education students, and
2 the monetary value of the waivers for the previous academic year.
3 (iii) The number of North American Indian students who receive a
4 granted waiver for the previous academic year.The monetary value of
5 the waivers granted each term for the previous academic year.
6 (iv) The number of graduate and undergraduate students
7 attending under a North American Indian tuition waiver who withdrew
8 from the public university each term during the previous academic
9 year. For purposes of this subparagraph, a withdrawal occurs when a
10 student who has been awarded the waiver withdraws from the
11 institution at any point during the term, regardless of enrollment
12 in subsequent terms.
13 (iv) (v) The number of graduate and undergraduate students
14 attending under a North American Indian tuition waiver who
15 successfully transfer to a 4-year public or private university, or
16 complete a degree or certificate program, separated by degree or
17 certificate level. , and the graduation rate for graduate and
18 undergraduate students attending under a North American Indian
19 tuition waiver who complete a degree or certificate within 150% of
20 the normal time to complete, separated by the level of the degree
21 or certificate.
22 (3) By January 1 of each year, a public university that
23 receives an appropriation in section 236, or a tribal college
24 receiving pass-through funds under section 269, 270, or 270c, shall
25 provide to the department of lifelong education, advancement, and
26 potential any information necessary for preparing the report
27 detailed in subsection (2), using guidelines and procedures

3 Sec. 276. (1) Included in the appropriation for fiscal year
4 2025-2026 2026-2027 for each public university in section 236 is
5 funding for the Martin Luther King, Jr. - Cesar Chavez Dolores
6 Huerta – Morris Hood Jr. - Rosa Parks future faculty fellowship
7 program that is intended to increase the pool of academically or
8 economically disadvantaged candidates pursuing faculty or
9 administration careers in postsecondary education in this state.
10 Preference may not be given to applicants on the basis of race,
11 color, ethnicity, gender, or national origin. Institutions should
12 encourage applications from applicants who would otherwise not
13 adequately be represented in the graduate student, faculty, or
14 administration populations. Each public university shall apply the
15 percentage change applicable to every public university in the
16 calculation of appropriations in section 236 to the amount of funds
17 allocated to the future faculty fellowship program.
18 (2) Each public university shall administer the program in a
19 manner prescribed by the department of labor and economic
20 opportunity. The department of labor and economic opportunity shall
21 use a good-faith effort standard to evaluate whether a fellowship
22 is in default. All of the following apply to the program:
23 (a) By June 15 of each year, public universities shall report
24 any anticipated unexpended or unencumbered program funds to the
25 department of labor and economic opportunity. Encumbered funds are
26 those funds that were committed by a fellowship agreement that is
27 signed during the current fiscal year or administrative expenses
28 that have been approved by the department of labor and economic
29 opportunity.

26 Sec. 295. The state budget director shall take steps to ensure
27 that all state fiscal recovery funds allocated to this state under
28 the American rescue plan act of 2021, Public Law 117-2,
29 are expended by December 31, 2026, as required by law. Any state
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

542
1 fiscal recovery funds that would otherwise lapse after September
2 30, 2026 are automatically reappropriated for the same purpose as
3 originally authorized and available for expenditure through
4 December 31, 2026 and any subsequent financial closeout period.
5 Sec. 295a. (1) The state budget director shall take steps to
6 ensure that all state fiscal recovery funds allocated to this state
7 under the American rescue plan act of 2021, Public Law 117-2, are
8 expended by December 31, 2026, as required by law. The state budget
9 director may reallocate appropriated funds for the purpose of fully
10 utilizing state fiscal recovery funds that are in jeopardy of not
11 meeting the expenditure deadline for reasons that may include, but
12 are not limited to, completed projects coming in under budget or
13 funds unable to be fully used by subrecipients. The state budget
14 director shall reallocate any of the funds reallocated under this
15 subsection to the programs or purposes specified in this section.
16 Any funds reallocated are unappropriated and immediately
17 reappropriated for the following purposes:
18 (a) To reclassify general fund/general purpose appropriations
19 for payroll and covered benefits for eligible public health and
20 safety employees at the department of corrections.
21 (b) To reclassify general fund/general purpose appropriations
22 for payroll and covered benefits for eligible public health and
23 safety employees at the department of state police.
24 (2) All applicable guidance, implementation, and reporting
25 provisions of Public Law 117-2 must be followed for state fiscal
26 recovery funds reallocated and reappropriated under subsection (1).
27 (3) The state budget director shall notify the senate and
28 house appropriations committees not later than 10 business days
29 after making any reallocations under subsection (1). The
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) CR-1 ct34ow

543
1 notification must include the authorized program under which funds
2 were originally appropriated, the amount of the reallocation, the

5 reallocated to each program or purpose.
